title: Bye-bye langweilige Teekannen\! Teapots mit neuem Design-Spin sind 2025 der Trend der Stunde
content:
**Teekannen im Trend: Von der funktionalen Kitchenware zum Designer-Highlight.**

Sie zeigen Ihre Teekanne und wir wissen, wer Sie sind? Ganz so weit ist es nun doch noch nicht gekommen. Schließlich denken wir hier nicht in Schubladen oder Kategorien. Und trotzdem dürfte die Wahl der Teekanne einen Funken Aufschluss darüber geben, welcher Geschmack dem Ihrem am nächsten kommt – seit diesem Jahr mehr denn je. Denn eine der interessantesten Beobachtungen der [wichtigsten Designmesse Salone del Mobile](https://www.ad-magazin.de/artikel/designtrends-salone-del-mobile){: target="_blank"} im April waren wundersame Teekannen, die vielmehr wie Kunst- und Designerpieces wirkten und auch sein wollten, als nur funktionale [Tableware](https://www.ad-magazin.de/artikel/geschirr-kollektionen-modemarken-trends){: target="_blank"}. Sie stellen Materialität, von Metall bis Keramik, in den Vordergrund. Sie weisen einzigartige Details vor, sind miteinander verbunden, gedreht, haben Füße (\!) oder wirken, als seien sie aus [Papier](https://www.ad-magazin.de/article/geschenkpapier){: target="_blank"}. Sie sind gelegentlich humorvoll und immer stilbewusst. Ist der aktuelle Hype um Teapots gerechtfertigt? Diese Modelle werden Sie davon überzeugen.



[#image: /photos/68889cc1a2f9523f1635b992]|||Küssen sich\! Natalia Criados „Mucura“ in versilbertem Messing, 1852 Euro.|||

+++feature-small

[#image: /photos/68889e8575013644e910fc24]|||„Ardilla“ heißt der Teapot, den Patricia Urquiola für Loewe erdachte – und sieht aus, wie nicht von dieser Welt.|||

+++

## Teekannen – damals bis heute
Die ersten Teekannen sind übrigens schon im 15. und 16. Jahrhundert entstanden. Yixing, eine Stadt in der chinesischen Provinz Jiangsu, wird als die Stadt, in der alles begann, gehandelt. Denn die Teapots, die schon damals die klassisch-typische, runde Form mit Griff und Ausguss aufwiesen, wurden aus dem für die Region charakteristischem violettem oder roten Ton gefertigt. Danach kam die Teekanne nach Europa, hinein in alle Haushalte. Der Rest ist Geschichte. Die Kitchenware umgibt große Tradition, spielt in einigen Kulturen eine wichtige Rolle (Teezeremonie\!) – als auch bei Luxusmodemarken. Seit dem 19. Jahrhundert produziert [Tiffany & Co.](https://www.ad-magazin.de/artikel/tiffany-store-duesseldorf-neueroeffnung){: target="_blank"} Teekannen, denke man nur an Elsa Perettis Versionen in Terrakotta und Schwarzglasur aus den 90er Jahren. Die postmoderne Keramikerin Dorothy Hafner wiederum entwarf für Rosenthal das Teeservice „Konfetti“.

### Diese Teapots sind wahre Designerpieces
Die Marke [Loewe](https://www.ad-magazin.de/artikel/jonathan-anderson-interview-loewe-flagship-store){: target="_blank"} hat es in Mailand wieder einmal geschafft, zu überraschen – und erstklassige Designer:innen für sich zu gewinnen. Willkommen bei der Teeparty – mit [David Chipperfield](https://www.ad-magazin.de/artikel/haus-von-david-chipperfield-kombiniert-robert-stephan-vintage-pieces-mit-zeitgenossischen-objekten){: target="_blank"}, der eine dunkelblaue, minimalistische Kanne mit Kesselhenkel designte, [Patricia Urquiola](https://www.ad-magazin.de/artikel/kuechen-kunstwerk){: target="_blank"}, deren lila-weißes Keramikmodell „Ardilla“ wie ein Ufo anmutet, Rosemarie Trockel, die einen silbernen Steinzeug-Teapot kreierte, und vielen mehr.



[#image: /photos/688a21068ab66f7bc30e80e8]|||Design von David Chipperfield für Loewe in Mitternachtsblau.|||



[#image: /photos/6888a10bd0ffb66a3b7a4e58]|||Teeservice von Dan MacCarthy für Loewe.|||

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/68889e4bd0ffb66a3b7a4e50 aspectRatio:3x4]|||Aus Steinzeug: Takayuki Sakiyamas „Choto's Listening to the Waves“-Teekanne, entworfen für Loewe.|||

[#image: /photos/68889e5dd0ffb66a3b7a4e52 aspectRatio:3x4]|||Dreht sich unentwegt: Takayuki Sakiyamas Teapot für Loewe.|||

+++



[#image: /photos/68889e76d0ffb66a3b7a4e54]|||Ein Hut? Teekanne\! Rosemarie Trockels Teekanne für Loewe.|||

## Formschön, humorvoll, geometrisch, organisch – Teekannen-Trends
Anissa Kermiche ist Schmuckdesignerin, entwirft aber auch Homeware. Was all ihre Designs gemeinsam haben? Sie zelebrieren die Form des weiblichen Körpers, sind humorvoll, organisch – und einfach erfrischend anders. Die niederländische Designerin Annebet Philips hat wiederum ein Service für Serax entworfen, das „Carte Blanche“ heißt und eckige Kartonformen auf Porzellan überträgt. Die Linien und Kanten sehen dabei aus, als wären sie mit schwarzem Stift nachgezeichnet. Natalia Criado ist sowieso der Star der Stunde und eroberte die Tableware-Trends mit ihren versilberten Messing-Cups, Tellern und Löffel im Sturm.



[#image: /photos/68889ec475013644e910fc26]|||Teekanne von Anissa Kermiche, die eine Frauenfigur formt.|||



[#image: /photos/6888a62cf8267f7b09927eae]|||Für Serax designte Annebet Philips Teekanne, Tassen, Milchkännchen und mehr.|||



[#image: /photos/68889eaaf8267f7b09927eaa]|||Im Silber-Trend: Natalia Criados „Teiera“-Kanne ist mit einem Rosenquarz besetzt.|||

### Reeditionen und Vintage-Revival
Nicht nur, was Möbel betrifft, wissen Marken ihr Erbe zu schätzen und wie sie damit zeitgemäß umgehen. In Sachen Tableware sind es Vintages und [Reeditionen](https://www.ad-magazin.de/artikel/reeditionen-im-wandel-design-klassiker-neu-aufgelegt){: target="_blank"}, die beliebter denn je sind – Christofle, WMF, Alessi sind nur einige der bekannten Namen in diesem Zusammenhang. Letztere Firma feiert nun jetzt 40-jähriges Jubiläum des „9093 Kettle“-Wasserkessels von Michael Graves. Deshalb kommt so ähnlich wie das Original, aber darüber hinaus auch ganz in Schwarz – und matt\! – daher.

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/6888a464d0ffb66a3b7a4e5a aspectRatio:1x1]|||Wie das Original von 1985: Wasserkessel aus Edelstahl, hochglanzpoliert, von Alessi.|||

[#image: /photos/6888a464f8267f7b09927eac]|||Die neumoderne Version der „9093 Kettle“ in Mattschwarz.|||

+++

+++feature-small

[#image: /photos/6888a6d1f8267f7b09927eb1]|||Ein Vintage-Highlight: Matteo Thuns Teekanne aus Chrom, entworfen in den 80ern für WMF, in eleganter, runder Form. Mit passendem Stövchen dazu, über [The Oblist](https://oblist.com/products/234299-chrome-teapot-by-matteo-thun-for-wmf){: target="_blank"}.|||

+++

### Teekannen im Natur-Look
Bambusreferenzen spielen schon bei Gucci seit jeher eine große Bedeutung. Este Ceramiche zeigt, dass der Bambus-Look hervorragend bei Teekannen, Tellern und Tassen funktioniert und für Sommer-Bohéme-Leichtigkeit sorgt. Das Gleiche gilt für Rattan-Designs – auch hier zeigt sich: Das Geflecht kommt nun nicht mehr nur als Sessel oder Ottoman, sondern auch als Teekanne mit passenden Teacups. Wenn sich der Sommer nicht zeigen will, dann holen wir ihn uns eben so herein. Darauf einen Tee-Toast\!



[#image: /photos/68889ee075013644e910fc28]|||Die „Bamboo“-Kollektion von Este Ceramiche ist handbemalt.|||



[#image: /photos/68889f1e75013644e910fc2a aspectRatio:5x7]|||Ideal fürs Picknick\! Teekanne und Cups aus Rattan, im 3er Set von Ferm Living.|||

title: Lebanese Artist Nathalie Harb’s Beirut Exhibition Is an Invitation To Dream
content:
For years Lebanese artist Nathalie Harb has viewed barren rooftops as more than just empty urban spaces: She is drawn to their untapped potential as destinations for making unexpected connections and letting loose, if only for a while. “Rooftops reminded me of the years I [discovered Beirut](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/beirut-a-city-guide-for-design-lovers){: target="_blank"} after the civil war, when I was in my 20s,” she says of her fascination with these omnipresent, but often neglected, areas. “I used to go into the city with friends and explore the abandoned houses and their rooftops. We would hang out there for moments of intimacy, for reading a book or sharing, and this was how I fell in love with the city.”

+++inset-right

[#image: /photos/6887697a4d6c98590f5b5ea1]

+++

Now, for her latest installation, *Pretend It’s a Pool*, the Lebanese artist and designer has tapped into the nostalgia of summers gone by. Collaborating with architect [Lea Kayrouz](https://www.instagram.com/leakayrouz/){: target="_blank"} and artist [Joseph Kai](https://www.instagram.com/josephkai/){: target="_blank"}, the showcase which has taken over the Beirut Art Center rooftop until September 20 is staged as a series of playful, improvised ‘micro-architectures’ that transform the concrete space from private to public; a love letter to [Beirut’s enduring way](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/with-a-resurgent-cultural-scene-beirut-is-all-set-to-get-back-on-its-feet){: target="_blank"} of bringing people together.

Looking out over the city, the dry river bed on one side, the chaos of Bourj Hammoud on the other, and the flea market below, this rooftop is like many in Beirut – a secret moment of perspective and calm suspended above the city’s woes and fatigue. Harb sought to bottle this feeling through colourful installations that function as rest stops – makeshift tents and day beds built from fabric, wood, breezeblocks and cushions – reminiscent of children’s [pillow](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/7-best-pillows-on-amazon-in-2025){: target="_blank"} forts.



[#externallink: /externallinks/6503041abb917946d989f4c1]



[#image: /photos/68876cd84d6c98590f5b5ea3]

## Must Read: [Beirut Exhibition *Impressions of Paradise* Is a Time Capsule That Transports You to the ‘Golden Age’ of Lebanon](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/beirut-exhibition-impressions-of-paradise-time-capsule-that-transports-you-to-the-golden-age-of-lebanon){: target="_blank"}
“At first we wanted to do a pool, because we wanted people to come and just be themselves, swim and be almost naked on this rooftop,” she explains. “Obviously, we couldn’t do a pool, so we created modular set-ups, inspired by the bustling street below, to make a kit that could create a tent or a sofa or something new, depending on how you assemble it.”

In addition to artistic interpretations of childhood pastimes, spare kits have been placed on the rooftop, allowing visitors to create their own set ups and become part of the installation. “From the outset, we were drawn to spaces on the margins that nonetheless hold potential as shared commons,” says Kayrouz. “We were also excited to stage familiar elements in unfamiliar settings, asking visitors to improvise, play and pretend.”



[#image: /photos/6887776ee3ece563e39678da]

Harb’s work, ranging from transforming car park roofs into gardens to installing sensory deprivation Silent Rooms in busy streets, has always centred on rethinking public space and how design could heal emotions. Many of her projects require a little vulnerability from the participants to make room for future dreams and possibilities to bloom. This is no different.

Reflecting poolside themes, the vibrant textile art that drapes the structures was developed by Kai; decorated with close-up drawings of bodies, zoomed in on eyes, shoulders and limbs, the fabrics play hide and seek with the onlooker. “Some references are clearly visible, while others fade into their weave, becoming a part of the textures of the installation,” he explains of the illustrations that evoke experiences tied to rooftop life, “The sensation of tanning, sitting on warm surfaces, getting goosebumps from the wind, or scanning the horizon… it’s all there. The body is not represented as whole, but dispersed across the site, absorbed by it.”



[#image: /photos/6887776ae3ece563e39678d7]

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/68877769e3ece563e39678d6]

[#image: /photos/6887776f4d6c98590f5b5eaa]

+++

Under Beirut’s skies, the ‘pool’ component in the installation is a small circular structure with wavy blue patterns – echoing the empty fountains dotted across the city, or buckets of water saved during the summer for when the tap water runs out. Visitors could even interpret it as a tub filled with water that kids would sit in to cool off – a core memory for many living in the Lebanese capital. In fact, Pretend It’s a Pool could be anything that evokes joy; all it takes is a little imagination. [*@nathaliehrb*](https://www.instagram.com/nathaliehrb/){: target="_blank"}

*Pretend It’s a Pool runs until September 20 at Beirut Art Center.*



[#externallink: /externallinks/650303f6bb917946d989f4c0]

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/6887776a4d6c98590f5b5ea5]

[#image: /photos/6887776f4d6c98590f5b5ea9]

+++



[#image: /photos/68877769bf1ce16f6c0586ec]



[#image: /photos/68876c9ae3ece563e39678d3]

title: Mais pourquoi reste-t-il si peu de momies ? La réponse est terrifiante
content:
[#image: /photos/6895d78d0127da0b47740b04]|||Les plus riches tombes égyptiennes étaient richement ornées. Le contenu même des momies, amulettes et bijoux, était souvent passé de main en main lors de soirées de déballage de momie organisées par de riches Victoriens.|||

Lorsque l’on demande quels sont les plus grands cannibales de l’histoire, la réponse est rarement la bonne. Certains diront, *« cro-magnon »*, et d’autres, *« les gens du moyen-âge »*, mais aucune de ces affirmations n’est vraie. Les plus grands cannibales de l’histoire étaient pour la majorité blancs, riches, et persuadés d’être très sophistiqués. Il s’agit bien sûr des [Victoriens](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/londres-40-m2-original-victorienne), avec leur obsession pour le macabre et leur soif d’exotisme. Plus sérieusement, une promenade au musée national d’histoire naturelle de Paris pourrait faire penser qu’il nous reste un grand nombre de momies à observer, et puis, pourquoi aurait-on besoin de plus, n’est-ce pas ? Nos sociétés occidentales sont bien heureuses quand les cadavres restent en terre, mais ça n’a pas toujours été le cas, et ces momies manquantes sont autant d'indices sur le mode de vie de nos ancêtres que nous ne retrouverons peut-être jamais.

### Une macabre panacée
Tout est parti d’une erreur de traduction. Le terme *« momie »* tel qu’on le connaît aujourd’hui, désigne un cadavre dans un état dessèchement extrême, ce qui a permis sa conservation au fil des années. Le mot désignait cependant autre chose à l’origine, une concoction à base d’épices et de résines naturelles utilisée depuis des siècles pour embaumer les corps, mais aussi comme panacée, c’est-à-dire médicament de tout mal, sous forme d’onguent, de poudre, ou de potion. Lorsqu’on observe les momies du [Louvre](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/renovation-musee-du-louvre), on se rend compte de plusieurs choses. La première, c’est que ces momies, aussi bien conservées et décorées soient-elles, sont pour la majorité des momies égyptiennes. La seconde, c’est que les corps à découvert ont une certaine apparence, et un brun caractéristique de l’assèchement extensif des tissus organiques.



[#image: /photos/6895d6b90127da0b47740b02]|||Grâce aux technologies modernes, il est désormais possible de savoir à quoi ressemblait l'homme caché sous ces couches de bandelettes. Les techniques utilisées par les embaumeurs étaient si complexe que les chercheurs auraient été incapables de le reconstituer leur travail à l'identique s'ils avaient tenté de le défaire.|||

Au fil de la visite du Louvre, on apprend que les anciens, et donc les [Égyptiens](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/egypte-maison-plage-futuriste-sphinx), croyaient peut-être plus encore que les chrétiens à l’immortalité de l’âme. Pour que celle-ci puisse atteindre le royaume des morts saine et sauve, le corps qui l’avait abrité durant la vie devait être embaumé avec le plus grand soin. Ainsi, après le décès, le corps était donc vidé, séché et parfumé pendant soixante-douze jours, jusqu’à être presque transformé en œuvre d’art comme la superbe momie enveloppée du Louvre avec sa mosaïque de bandelettes autour du visage. Bien sûr, toutes les castes de la société n’avaient pas le droit aux momifications les plus poussées, mais chacun pouvait accéder à l’au-delà, car c’était dans la nature de l’âme. Plus la vie avait été vertueuse, plus la pesée de l’âme par Osiris, le Seigneur des Morts de la [mythologie](https://www.admagazine.fr/adactualites/article/visionnaire-re-sacralise-la-decoration-interieure-avec-mythica) égyptienne, serait en la faveur du défunt. Avec toutes ces informations, on peut inférer que des milliers de personnes, mais aussi d’animaux et d'objets ont été momifiés au fil des siècles en Egypte, mais aussi dans le reste du monde. On peut aussi ajouter à ce nombre les momies dites naturelles, c'est-à-dire des corps momifiés non pas par la main de l’homme, mais celle des éléments, ce qui rend le nombre de momies restantes étonnant, et même suspect.

### Le commerce médicinal de la mort
Pendant l’[ère victorienne](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/styles-maisons-anglaises-caracteristiques), l’occident découvre les merveilles du reste du monde avec une frénésie de consommation que l’on ne connaît que trop bien, et l’Egypte, avec ses milliers d’années d’histoire et de trésors, fascine. Au total, le [musée national d’histoire naturelle](https://www.admagazine.fr/decoration/shopping/article/carriere-freres-museum-national-histoire-naturelle-collection-de-bougies) de Paris possède une large collection de momies. Selon le site officiel du musée, *« la collection abrite 63 momies complètes, dont 33 Égyptiennes, 23 Sud-Américaines et 7 d'autres origines, ainsi que 52 têtes momifiées isolées venant aussi d'Egypte et d'Amérique du Sud, et divers autres fragments de corps ».* Parmi ces momies, des cadavres humains, bien sûr, mais aussi des crocodiles, des oiseaux, et des chats. Entre les années 1880 et 1890, environ 300 mille momies de chat, des animaux adorés par les Égyptiens pour leur connexion à la déesse Bastet, ont été réduites en poudre pour servir d’engrais agricole. Mais pourquoi donc ? Parce que la momie—appelée *« mumia »*, *« mummia »*, etc—faisait fureur sur le marché occidental. Les riches victoriens s'arrachaient les momies achetées en voyages ou arrachées à leurs tombes, que ça soit pour en faire des spectacles comme lors des soirées de déballage de momies de Thomas Pettigrew, auto-proclamé expert des momies égyptiennes, ou pour en faire un médicament supposé guérir tous les maux.



[#image: /photos/6895d70c74ef365b53f05791]|||Si la momie de chat du Louvre est moins spectaculaire que celle-ci, exposée au British Museum, il reste aujourd'hui une quantité considérable de momies de chat visible dans divers musées, ce qui souligne l'amour que portaient les anciens à leurs compagnons à fourrure.|||

Si la consommation de momies, ou du moins de *«mumia »* est introduite après les croisades, la tendance est devenue si populaire à l’époque victorienne qu’une distinction est même apparue entre *« mumia nera »* et *« mumia blanca »*. La première était la poudre de momie noire ou brune, généralement à partir d’un être humain adulte, comme la Momie de Padiimenipet au Louvre, alors que la *« mumia blanca »* avait été préparée à partir de chair blanche, soit disant ayant appartenu à de jeunes filles vierges. Pourquoi parler de virginité alors que n’importe quoi et n’importe qui aurait pu être momifié ? Car le marché était en plein essor, et l’une serait réputée plus efficace que l’autre pour traiter telle ou telle maladie. Dans ce contexte, la demande était énorme, trop même, pour que le marché ne soit pas envahi par la contrefaçon. Ainsi, si le brun de momie, un [pigment](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/peinture-nouvelles-couleurs) populaire à l’époque, était soit disant conçu à partir de poudre de momie, les études de peintures confectionnées avec ces pigments ne contiennent que rarement, sinon jamais de réelle momie ou même d’ingrédients fixes. De la même façon, une grande quantité de momies en circulation sur le marché n’en avait que le nom, car elle ne contenait pas de momie égyptienne, ou même d’épices qui avaient à l’origine fait le succès de la momie. Les escrocs se contentaient de piller des tombes ou de subtiliser des corps sur le gibet pour les faire sécher avec plus ou moins de soin et les vendre au prix de la vraie momie à leurs clients. Lorsqu’on voit la collection des [musées](https://www.admagazine.fr/architecture/balade/diaporama/les-30-musees-les-plus-spectaculaires-dans-le-monde/61537) de France et du monde, il est difficile de penser que des artefacts précieux comme le superbe sarcophage de Ramsès III auraient pu finir dans une de ces soirées où les convives se rassemblaient pour déballer une momie comme un cadeau à Noël…



[#image: /photos/6895d87174ef365b53f05793]|||Pour décourager le pillage des tombes anciennes, les pays qui en possédaient beaucoup dont l'Egypte faisaient circuler des rumeurs de malédictions comme celle du tombeau de Toutankhamon. Malheureusement, cela n'a fait qu'attiser la convoitise des Victoriens qui se racontaient des histoires de fantômes tout en déballant des momies pillées en Egypte.|||

title: The Right Way To Charge Your Clients
content:
When it comes to charging strategies, architects and interior designers across the Middle East are sharing their insights. After all, more transparency into the pricing strategy of successful design practices offers not only supportive guidance to industry peers but can also elevate the perceived value of the profession at large.

Pricing remains a topic of major interest across the regional design industry. Most designers use one of several charging strategies: a flat fee, an hourly rate, cost-plus models, or hybrid approaches. But how do you decide which strategy works best for your practice in the Middle East's unique market? We consulted leading regional designers to understand their decision-making process and the cultural considerations that shape their pricing strategies.

## Determining Your Rate
Before choosing a specific charging model, the fundamental question remains the same across markets: how much do you want to earn, and how much do you want to work? This top-down approach, rather than building from the bottom up, forms the foundation of sustainable pricing.

"We use a time-based pricing model rooted in the resources we allocate, factoring in design hours, overheads, and a reasonable profit margin," explains Pallavi Dean of Roar Design. This resource-deployment approach ensures that fees reflect actual studio capacity and expertise rather than arbitrary percentages.

For Fadi Sarieddine of Fadi Sarieddine Design Studio, the approach is similarly methodical: "We follow a fee strategy based on the size of the team involved and the time required, which will be marked with a profit percentage." This ensures that pricing scales appropriately with project complexity and team expertise.

## The Evolution of Pricing Models
Many practices find their pricing strategies evolving as they grow. Tarik Al Zaharna of T.ZED Architects describes this journey: "In the early days, we based our pricing strategy on different stages of design deliverables, scope of work, etc. We tried to peg our fees against a percentage of the construction cost."

However, operating across emerging Middle Eastern economies presented unique challenges. "It's not always a standard construction cost that's applied to different typologies of projects. If we adopt a very rigid approach to pricing, sometimes it deters a client. The flexibility that's very common in the Middle East translates into professional practice."

This led to adaptations: "Previously, we would price a fixed fee per square meter of the project. Depending on the scale, our fee per square meter would go up for smaller projects or go down for larger ones." As practices expand and take on more complex projects involving MEP, structural engineering, and regulatory approvals, fee structures must evolve accordingly.

## Managing Expanded Scope and Regional Adaptation
As practices grow, so does their responsibility. "We're architects and interior architects, but we've been tasked with including mechanical, electrical, plumbing, structural engineering, and handling approvals," notes Al Zaharna. "The scope increased, and we had to go to sub-consultants to get their fees, package that into our single proposal, and go back to the client."

For commercial projects, this complexity is particularly pronounced. Dean explains: "Commercial clients often require additional services like MEP, acoustics, or fire and life safety, which naturally impacts the total fee." However, the fundamental pricing approach remains consistent: "Our pricing structure remains the same across both [residential and commercial]. It all comes down to the time spent in the studio and the deliverables required."

The Middle Eastern market also presents unique cultural considerations that impact pricing strategies. Al Zaharna observes: "There's definitely a bartering culture. We submit our fees or proposal understanding and knowing that there would be negotiations. So we inflate a little bit, maybe 5%, and then the minute they get a discount is when they're happy."

Sarieddine notes regional service expectations: "Working in Dubai and across the Middle East, we've noticed that clients really value a high level of service, flexibility, and quick turnaround times. That naturally impacts how we price our projects as we have to factor in the extra coordination, custom requests, and often, sourcing special materials that aren't always locally available."

## The Hybrid Model
Most successful Middle Eastern practices have gravitated toward hybrid models that combine multiple charging strategies. Al Zaharna's current approach illustrates this: "Based on construction cost understanding and scale, we'll charge anywhere between 7 to 12% of the construction costs, depending on scope of work, timeline, budget, etc."

For procurement services, an additional layer is added: "If the client wants us to procure, we would charge 10-15% of the contract value to manage the procurement." This separation of design and procurement services allows for transparency while ensuring designers are compensated for the additional coordination required.

This transparent approach becomes particularly important given one of the most significant challenges facing designers in the region: maintaining pricing integrity in a market where kickbacks and commissions are prevalent. Al Zaharna addresses this directly: "The biggest hurdle is that the culture of kickbacks in the Middle East is so prevalent. A lot of architects would come in and lowball everybody's fees. They'd charge 2-3% for design and say, 'We'll do it all.' But then in their contract, they'll lock the client into using their contractor, their suppliers."

The response from ethical practices is a clear separation of services. Dean states: "Some competitors in the region adopt a commission-based model tied to the supply chain, but we intentionally separate design from procurement. Our integrity is non-negotiable. We want clients to know we're specifying what's right for them, not because it's tied to a financial incentive."

## Project Management and Payment Protection
Clear boundaries around revisions and timelines are crucial for maintaining profitability. Al Zaharna's approach includes specific contractual protections: "In our agreements, we say concept design will take three weeks, for example. There's a note that says this timeline excludes any time the client takes for review and feedback."

When changes occur, variation orders become essential. "If there is a written and formal request by the client to add or change things, the timeline changes, and we issue a variation notice saying, 'You've asked for additional work. It's different from what we discussed and agreed. Therefore, we're going to charge you extra.'"

Dean echoes this sentiment: "Design is an iterative process, and we embrace that. We educate our clients early that they're welcome to change their minds, but each change has an impact on timelines, on coordination, and sometimes on fees."

Successful practices also implement clear payment schedules that protect both parties. Sarieddine's approach is typical: "We typically break it down into three main phases: Initial Payment (30%) upon signing, Mid-Stage Payment (40%) once design direction is approved, and Final Payment (30%) due before final handover."

Dean emphasises the importance of upfront commitment: "We always include a mobilisation fee upfront. It's a good litmus test for client commitment, especially in the early stages when some may sign and then reconsider."

For uncertain projects, pre-concept packages offer additional protection. Al Zaharna explains: "For clients who approach us with plots of land but don't want to over-commit before they get approvals, we do what we call a pre-concept package or feasibility study. We charge a fixed fee based on a fixed duration and number of revisions. If they appoint us for the full project, we'll deduct that fee from our full scope fees."

## Luxury Projects and Customisation
Ultra-luxury projects require adjusted approaches while maintaining core principles. Sarieddine notes: "Ultra-luxury projects will naturally require more time and a higher-skilled talent, so ultimately their fee requirements will reflect accordingly."

However, the fundamental approach remains consistent: "We intentionally and fairly separate our fee from the project cost. We believe that our effort does not differ whether certain accessories are golden or stainless steel."

For customisation-heavy projects, time estimation becomes crucial. Sarieddine explains: "When a project involves heavy customisation, we usually start by breaking the design down into components and estimating how much added time each will require in terms of design development, sampling, approvals, and coordination."

Transparency becomes particularly important in this context, as regional clients value knowing exactly what they're paying for. "Clients appreciate knowing exactly what they're paying for and having the freedom to make changes along the way. So instead of sticking to a rigid pricing model, we prefer to keep things flexible and tailor our approach to each project and client," Sarieddine adds.

## Popular Pricing Models in the Region
While hybrid approaches dominate, several distinct models have proven successful across different practice types and scales in the Middle East:

**Time-Based Pricing** remains the foundation for most established practices. "We use a time-based pricing model rooted in the resources we allocate, factoring in design hours, overheads, and a reasonable profit margin," explains Dean. This model scales naturally with project complexity and team expertise requirements.

**Construction Cost Percentage** offers predictability for larger projects. Al Zaharna's practice charges "anywhere between 7 to 12% of the construction costs, depending on scope of work, timeline, budget," with additional procurement fees of "10-15% of the contract value" when handling purchasing.

**Fixed-Scope Packages** provide clarity for specific project phases. Al Zaharna's pre-concept approach (charging "a fixed fee based on a fixed duration and number of revisions") protects both parties during uncertain early stages while maintaining the option to credit fees toward full project engagement.

The most successful practices often combine elements from each model, adapting their approach based on project type, client relationship, and scope complexity.

## Finding Your Strategy
There's no universal formula for pricing in the Middle East's diverse market, but successful practices share common principles. The foundation lies in understanding your actual costs, recognising the value you deliver, and adapting to regional expectations while maintaining professional integrity.

Most established practices find that hybrid models work best, combining time-based fees with project-specific considerations like scope complexity, client service levels, and market positioning. The key is developing a system that's both flexible enough to accommodate the region's business culture and structured enough to protect your profitability and ensure consistent quality delivery.

## Top Tips for Pricing Success
**Never work for free.** As Fareed Esmaeil of X Architects emphasises: "No unpaid competitions, no free concepts. Each phase—research, concept, development—should have clear deliverables and a defined fee."

**Document everything.** "We document every project: how much time each stage took, how many team members were involved, what was delivered, and how it performed financially," explains Esmaeil. This creates an evolving benchmark for future pricing.

**Be specific about deliverables.** Al Zaharna stresses: "You have to be really pedantic about the deliverables: the number of layouts, reflected ceiling plans, sections, and where you're going to cut the sections. The number of visuals is a big one."

**Separate creative and administrative roles.** "It's hard to juggle both roles at once. The architect stays focused on the creative direction, while the project manager handles scope tracking, logs changes, and communicates with the client," notes Esmaeil.

**Build in a negotiation buffer.** Understanding regional bartering culture, many successful practices factor in a 5-10% buffer for negotiations while maintaining their core fee requirements.

**Prioritise transparency over kickbacks.** In a region where relationships and trust drive business, transparent pricing strategies that prioritise client value over commission systems ultimately build stronger, more sustainable practices.

title: This 6,500-square-foot Mumbai duplex is quiet, warm, and filled with subtle textures
content:
The residents of this Mumbai duplex presented a very succinct brief to Palak Kothari and Rashmi Shinde—principal architect and project lead designer respectively—at Orchestrated Chambers. The family wanted a home that was [minimal](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/minimalist-homes-in-india-that-are-quiet-retreats-in-nature/){: target="_blank"} and clutter-free, something that was easy to take care of. To this brief, Kothari and Shinde added the stipulation that the home, despite being minimal, must be full of quiet character. The result is a home that straddles both worlds effortlessly, and in the words of Kothari is, “comfortable, stylish, and built to last.”



[#image: /photos/68ac27073ba49e6db7b8cf2e]

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/68ac217b394ce10bd00ebaa3]

[#image: /photos/68ac217bb2f615de483e45a2]

+++

*Also read: [This 2,800-square-foot apartment in South Mumbai celebrates fluid minimalism](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/this-2800-square-foot-apartment-in-south-mumbai-celebrates-fluid-minimalism/){: target="_blank"}*

Kothari and Shinde decided to use textural minimalism to infuse character into the home, while still maintaining its simplicity. To achieve this, selecting the correct material palette was of the utmost importance. It started with a neutral beige base, to which they added the raw industrial allure of metal, and the rugged beauty of granite that is softened by the natural warmth and depth of wood; soft fabric furnishings and lime washed walls round off this wonderfully tactile palette without being visually oppressive. As Kothari explains, “Each material has been selected with intention, never overpowering, and always enhancing the warmth and quiet elegance of the space.”



[#image: /photos/68ac21c84cfcaece4e2b8f48]

Another way in which the designers added visual depth and character to the space is through the addition of curves that guide the eye. They make subtle appearances in the living room through the organic form of the couch, the slightly recessed arches on the walls, and the soft arches that carve out an intimate dining area separate from the living room. This use of curves and materiality coalesce beautifully in the design of the staircase that leads to the upper floor of the home. A vision in red, this sculptural staircase curves upwards, with the natural veining of its steps adding another layer of texture to the space. “This bold material contrast gives the staircase a quiet strength, anchoring the softness of its form,” Kothari adds.



[#image: /photos/68ac2238b2f615de483e45a4]

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/68ac2346394ce10bd00ebaa7]

[#image: /photos/68ac25e58eb5bc2a72bcd152]

+++

*Also read: [This sea-facing Mumbai home unfolds with a gentle, light-washed openness](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/this-sea-facing-mumbai-home-unfolds-with-a-gentle-light-washed-openness/){: target="_blank"}*

The staircase not only grounds the palette of this home, but also leads to what is arguably, the heart of this home. As Kothari describes it, “Every space in the home is intuitively placed, allowing for privacy while preserving the openness that defines the home’s character.” This is exemplified by the design of the [den](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/3-cosy-den-designs-that-will-turn-your-entertainment-room-into-a-welcoming-retreat/){: target="_blank"}, a space designed to be a private retreat, but also connected to the terrace and outdoor bar for entertaining guests. Here too, the materiality of the home shines through with the ombré metallic bar taking centre stage.



[#image: /photos/68ac23a44cfcaece4e2b8f4a]

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/68ac25234cfcaece4e2b8f4e]

[#image: /photos/68ac23f84cfcaece4e2b8f4c]

+++

The bedrooms of this Mumbai duplex are evenly divided amongst the two floors, with the lower level consisting of two bedrooms as well as a guest room and kitchen. The upper level is more secluded– consisting of the den, primary bedroom, as well as children’s room. In the primary bedroom, textural minimalism makes another appearance in the form of a claypaste wall that evokes an organic rock-like texture.

The designers aptly named this project ‘The Material Poem’, as it is deeply entrenched in materiality, with subtle textural nuances found in every corner of the home. ‘The Material Poem’ is not only a succinct physical description of the home, but also a promise to do justice to every element within it.

*Also read: [This apartment in Mumbai is a poetic sanctuary designed to escape the chaos of the city](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/this-apartment-in-mumbai-is-a-poetic-sanctuary-designed-to-escape-the-chaos-of-the-city/){: target="_blank"}*

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/68ac260d4cfcaece4e2b8f50]

[#image: /photos/68ac260c3ba49e6db7b8cf2c]

+++

*Styling by Altrove.*

title: Inside Prince William and Kate Middleton's $22 million new home, Forest Lodge
content:
After months of speculation, Prince William and Kate Middleton are finally on the move, but they're not venturing too far from their current home, Adelaide Cottage: the Prince and Princess of Wales's new home, Forest Lodge, is also in Windsor, and many have already billed it as the young royal family's “forever home.”

William, Kate and their children have faced a series of challenges since they moved into Adelaide Cottage three years ago: Queen Elizabeth II's death soon after their settled in, Catherine's cancer treatment and King Charles' cancer diagnosis. Earlier this year there were reports about a security breach following a break-in on the estate grounds while the Waleses were in residence. Given the circumstances, it is easy to understand the family's desire for a change of scene and like it is for any family, William and Kate's new home will mark [a fresh start](https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cpwyk1v0p8yo){: target="_blank"}. Here is everything we know about Forest Lodge.

## Where is William and Kate’s new home?


[#image: /photos/68aecdb90a433e43e49fa732]

Nestled in a private and calm corner of Windsor Great Park, Forest lodge is just four miles from William and Kate's current home, Adelaide Cottage. Built in the 1770s, it is a Georgian mansion with eight bedrooms, a massive upgrade for the couple. It is also a practical choice for the hands-on couple who are often seen accompanying their children on the school run: Prince George, Princess Charlotte and Prince Louis attend Lambrook School which remains just a short drive away.



[#image: /photos/68aecdb90a433e43e49fa733]

## Who designed Forest Lodge?


[#image: /photos/68aecdb90a433e43e49fa734]

Surrounded by woodlands in the 4,800-acre Windsor Great Park, Forest Lodge was designed by Humphry Repton. Its red-brick facade conceals a half-barrel vaulted entrance hall and all the trimmings of a grand Georgian-era mansion: ornate plasterwork and cornicing, marble fireplaces and venetian windows that overlook the verdant setting. Respectfully enlarged and restored over the years, it was acquired by the Crown in 1829 and since March 1972 has enjoyed Grade II-listed status.

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/68aecdb90a433e43e49fa735]

[#image: /photos/68aecdb90a433e43e49fa736]

+++

## How big is William and Kate’s new home?
Compared to the four-bedroom Adelaide Cottage, William and Kate Middleton's new home, Forest Lodge, has eight bedrooms. It features six chimneys and nine bay windows. And while their new home has the space, as is the case with their [family set-up](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/a-family-home-in-goa-where-a-courtyard-and-100-year-old-columns-add-to-its-charm-studio-415/){: target="_blank"} at Adelaide Cottage, William and Kate will have no live-in staff.

## How much is Forest Lodge worth today?


[#image: /photos/68aecdb90a433e43e49fa737]

After its last renovation in 2001, which costed approximately $2 million, the mansion was valued at $7.45 million. If Forest Lodge were to come on the market today, it would be worth approximately $22 million.



[#image: /photos/68aecdb90a433e43e49fa738]

## Who lived at Forest Lodge before William and Kate?
Notable former occupants of Forest Lodge include Sir Malcom Murray, Sir William Fremantle and Sir John Aird. In 1998, you could rent it for $82,000 a year. After it was announced that the Prince and Princess of Wales were relocating to Forest Lodge, news circulated that two families who lived in cottages nearby were asked to vacate. To offer context, these families rented the cottages (once Forest Lodge's stables) from the Crown Estate and have been offered similar accommodations elsewhere in Windsor Great Park.

## Who will pay the rent for William and Kate’s new home, Forest Lodge?
William and Kate will pay the rent for Forest Lodge personally, meaning, they will not be using public funds. Any minor [renovations](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/ad-revisits-old-school-homes-that-remind-us-of-our-nani-ka-ghar/){: target="_blank"} will also be paid for by the couple.

## Why are William and Kate moving to Forest Lodge?


[#image: /photos/68aecdb90a433e43e49fa739]

A chance for a new start following a stressful few years, William and Kate's new home is also a strategic move. After the family has settled into life in Windsor, Forest Lodge is being billed as their forever home: a place where Prince George, Princess Charlotte and Prince Louis will grow up, and as importantly, where they may [continue to live](https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cpwyk1v0p8yo){: target="_blank"} when the Prince and Princess of Wales become King and Queen. In that respect, Forest Lodge is regal enough without being ostentatious, a line William and Kate toe so effortlessly.

While [Buckingham Palace](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/gallery/the-worlds-most-expensive-home-the-buckingham-palace/){: target="_blank"} has remained the British monarch's formal home for generations, in her final years Queen Elizabeth II primarily resided at Windsor Castle. King Charles continues to maintain residence at Clarence House, where he lived with Camilla before he acceded to the throne in September 2022. Currently undergoing phased restoration, Buckingham Palace is used for formal events, receptions and other business of the state. Prince William and Kate Middleton's move to Forest Lodge is an indication that they plan to continue this new tradition.

## When will William and Kate move into their new home?
It is expected that William and Kate will move their family into Forest Lodge before Christmas this year.

title: Les 5 villes les plus relaxantes à visiter dans le monde en 2025
content:
**Découvrez le classement des cinq villes les plus relaxantes au monde en 2025.**

Imaginez une ville, n’importe laquelle. Pour beaucoup d’entre nous, cela évoque aussitôt des [gratte-ciel](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/nouveaux-gratte-spectaculaires-france), des klaxons et des foules pressées. C’est sans doute passionnant et exaltant, mais guère *« relaxant »*. Certes, on peut toujours y dénicher des coins paisibles – qu’il s’agisse de vastes espaces verts, de toits-terrasses ou de spas souterrains – mais pour qu’une ville soit officiellement considérée comme *« relaxante »*, elle doit répondre à plusieurs critères.

Le fournisseur international d’eSIM Holafly a publié des données qui s’attaquent précisément à cette question : quelles villes peuvent réellement être considérées comme relaxantes ? L’étude prend en compte de nombreux aspects, allant de l’accès aux réserves [naturelles](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/cavernes-naturelles-spectaculaires-monde) à la qualité de l’air, en passant par le nombre de centres de bien-être, la moyenne d’heures d’ensoleillement (d’après World Weather & Climate), les embouteillages et même le niveau général de bonheur de la population (selon l’indice *Happy City Index 2025*), afin d’établir une liste complète.

Le bien-être occupe une place de plus en plus importante pour les voyageurs d’aujourd’hui. Avec la croissance constante du secteur, un nombre croissant de personnes placent désormais la santé et la détente au cœur de leurs projets de [voyage](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/11-grandes-tendances-voyage-2025). Voici le classement des cinq villes les plus relaxantes au monde en 2025.



[#image: /photos/68b1ba2f3fd136a488734c05]

### 5\. Melbourne, Australie
Cette ville australienne figure régulièrement parmi les meilleures au monde. Melbourne a occupé la première place du Global Liveability Index de l’Economist Intelligence Unit pendant cinq années consécutives et se maintient aujourd’hui à la quatrième. Réputée pour sa gastronomie – entre cafés de classe mondiale, charmants [restaurants](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/meilleurs-restaurants-peniches-paris) de quartier et une multitude de lieux culturels – elle incarne un véritable art de vivre hédoniste et décontracté. La ville compte également 215 parcs et réserves naturelles, 249 spas et centres de bien-être, et profite de 2 360 heures d’ensoleillement par an. Rien d’étonnant, donc, à ce qu’elle figure dans ce classement.

### 4\. Sydney, Australie
Sydney, qui occupe la quatrième place, devance de justesse Melbourne. Quelques jours suffisent pour comprendre pourquoi la ville figure si haut dans le classement. Ici, les habitants se lèvent à l’aube pour rejoindre la [côte](https://www.admagazine.fr/galerie/plus-belles-villes-cotieres), où ils passent leurs matinées à surfer, marcher ou courir le long du littoral – une recette infaillible pour relâcher les tensions et cultiver la détente. Avec 2 468 heures d’ensoleillement par an, la vie se déroule largement en plein air, et ses 239 parcs offrent autant de possibilités de profiter de la nature.



[#image: /photos/68b1ba2f3fd136a488734c06]|||Vienne abrite plus de 100 galeries et musées différents proposant une offre culturelle très riche.|||

### 3\. Vienne, Autriche
Passer un après-midi dans une galerie ou un musée a quelque chose de profondément apaisant pour l’esprit – et [Vienne](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/appartement-artiste-richard-peduzzi-vienne) en compte plus de cent parmi lesquels choisir. Classée troisième dans ce palmarès de la détente, la capitale autrichienne affiche l’un des meilleurs résultats en matière de qualité de l’air. Elle bénéficie en effet d’une atmosphère parmi les plus pures d’Europe, grâce à des infrastructures intelligentes et à des politiques respectueuses de l’environnement. Haut lieu culturel et ville résolument progressiste, Vienne mérite sans conteste une place sur votre liste de destinations à visiter en 2025.

### 2\. Singapour
Si vous aimez les chiffres, en voici un qui devrait vous plaire : [Singapour](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/singapour-villes-plus-vertes-monde) compte 242 parcs, 698 spas et centres de bien-être, et profite de 2 022 heures d’ensoleillement par an. Réputée dans le monde entier pour son engagement écologique – avec des plans ambitieux visant à verdir la cité-État d’ici 2030 – elle est souvent surnommée la *« ville-jardin »* grâce à ses innombrables parcs et espaces verts. Elle s’est également classée cinquième dans notre propre *Readers’ Choice Awards* des villes les plus accueillantes au monde. Et quoi de mieux, pour apaiser les voyageurs fatigués, que des habitants souriants et chaleureux ?



[#image: /photos/68b1ba2f3fd136a488734c07]|||Avec son ambiance décontractée et son accès facile aux grands espaces, [San Diego](https://www.admagazine.fr/decoration/visite-deco/diaporama/californie-une-maison-sur-la-falaise/35894) arrive en tête de la liste des villes les plus relaxantes au monde.|||

### 1\. San Diego, États-Unis
Dans le plus pur esprit californien, San Diego se hisse en tête de ce classement des villes les plus relaxantes. Ici, tout invite aux grands espaces : plages et côtes spectaculaires, randonnées et sentiers épiques à l’intérieur des terres. La ville compte plus de parcs et réserves naturelles que n’importe quelle autre de la liste (266). Rendez-vous, par exemple, à la réserve naturelle d’État de Torrey Pines pour profiter de vues imprenables et de balades sans foule, ou à la [plage](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/belle-plage-monde-bretagne-belle-ile-en-mer) d’État de San Onofre, véritable paradis des surfeurs, avant d’apaiser vos muscles et de vous détendre dans l’un des 359 spas et centres de bien-être de la ville.

Article initialement publié dans [*Condé Nast Traveller*](https://www.cntraveller.com/article/its-official-these-are-the-worlds-most-relaxing-cities-to-visit-in-2025){: target="_blank"}.

title: 5 idées pour recréer le jardin des fiançailles de Taylor Swift et Travis Kelce
content:
Tout le monde est fasciné par la bague en diamant taille coussin de la future mariée, mais c’est le jardin à l’anglaise de [Taylor Swift](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/taylor-swift-portefeuille-immobilier) et Travis Kelce, aperçu sur leurs photos de fiançailles, qui nous donne surtout envie d’en voir davantage. Plus de joie, plus de romantisme et, bien sûr, plus d’inspiration pour le jardinage.

C’est un décor à plusieurs niveaux, mais pas trop rigide ; il y a de la couleur, mais juste ce qu’il faut, et ce qui lui manque en formalité, il le compense par un style subtil (non, nous ne parlons pas de ce grand lustre à fleurs). Voici tout ce que nous aimons – et tout ce que vous devez savoir – pour recréer le jardin de fiançailles de Taylor Swift et [Travis Kelce](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/travis-kelce-portefeuille-immobilier-fiance-taylor-swift), où que vous soyez.



[#image: /photos/68bb03012c3bdd2586c5d1f3]|||Une photo d’archive des jardins du Smithsonian, illustrant l’attrait intemporel des jardins de style cottage.|||

## 1\. Privilégiez les couleurs douces
Si vous souhaitez créer un jardin cottage anglais à partir de zéro, le mot d’ordre est *« douceur »* : c’est le style à privilégier. Évitez les couleurs vives et intenses comme le rouge, l’orange ou le jaune éclatant. Orientez-vous plutôt vers des tons pastel – rose pâle, bleu ciel, lilas et lavande – pour commencer. Ajoutez ensuite des touches de blanc, de jaune beurre et des plantes au feuillage argenté. Laissez ces [plantes](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/plantes-rustiques-13-fleurs-robustes-jardin) s’installer pendant un an avant d’apporter de la profondeur avec des nuances de violet foncé et de jaune soleil, toujours avec modération. Considérez ces couleurs plus profondes comme des points focaux : elles attirent le regard vers d’autres zones ou allongent la ligne de vue, créant ainsi l’illusion d’un jardin plus vaste en les plantant tout au fond.

## 2\. Ne réfléchissez pas trop à votre schéma de plantation
Le charme d’un jardin de [cottage](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/canada-cottage-lac-%C3%A9rables) réside dans son aspect sauvage, comme si la nature l’avait toujours voulu ainsi. Nous savons tous que les looks les plus simples sont souvent les plus difficiles à obtenir, mais ces conseils devraient vous aider.

En général, on plante les végétaux les plus hauts à l’arrière et on réduit progressivement la hauteur en se rapprochant du bord de la bordure. Oubliez cette règle et mélangez les hauteurs, à condition de rester raisonnable et de vous assurer que toutes les plantes bénéficient de lumière naturelle. Vous pouvez associer des plantes hautes et vaporeuses, regroupées en [bouquets](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/pourquoi-personnes-de-bon-gout-choisissent-compositions-florales-discretes), à une bordure basse, jouer sur le contraste des formes de feuilles et même des couleurs.

Pour un véritable jardin de style cottage, plantez en groupes et plus serré que ne le suggèrent les instructions. La terre nue n’est pas très esthétique, mais il est judicieux de laisser quelques espaces soigneusement dissimulés entre les plantes imposantes afin d’avoir de la place pour en ajouter d’autres l’année suivante. Vous pouvez également élargir légèrement votre bordure ou, pourquoi pas, oublier complètement la notion de bordure : créez des allées sinueuses en [gravier](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/idees-deco-jardin-gravier) et laissez vos plantes effleurer vos vêtements lorsque vous vous promenez.

## 3\. Sélectionnez les bonnes espèces de fleurs
Les bases de tout jardin de style cottage sont les classiques : hortensias *« Annabelle »*, pivoines et toutes sortes de rosiers – arbustifs, grimpants ou lianes. Pour ajouter de la hauteur, choisissez des pieds-d’alouette, des lupins, de l’ail d’ornement et des anémones, et apportez du mouvement avec des gauras et des sauges.

N’oubliez pas le feuillage. Un mélange d’arbustes à feuilles persistantes et de plantes vivaces apporte texture et variations de couleurs tout en permettant au [jardin](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/jardin-a-la-francaise) d’évoluer au fil des saisons. Investir dans des plantes à écorce rouge ou aux feuilles plus foncées aidera à maintenir l’intérêt tout au long de l’année.

## 4\. Apportez de la hauteur à votre jardin


[#image: /photos/68bb03012c3bdd2586c5d1f4]

Le jardin de Travis (c’est là qu’il a demandé Taylor en mariage dans sa maison du Kansas) était doté d’une structure métallique imposante, devenue le point central de la décoration, apportant à la fois hauteur et espace. Investissez dans une arche de [roses](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/belles-fleurs-roses-printemps) ou un treillis pour y faire grimper une plante grimpante, comme la passiflore ou la clématite. Creusez un petit trou pour y fixer la structure ou installez-la dans des pots en terre cuite, en les entourant d’arbustes pour la mettre en valeur.

## 5\. Vous manquez d’espace ? Optez pour un jardin en pot
Vous rêvez d’un jardin à l’anglaise, mais vous n’avez ni l’espace ni le temps pour le réaliser ? Recréez ce style sur un balcon ou une terrasse grâce au jardin en pot. Procurez-vous une série de [pots](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/que-mettre-grands-pots-terrasse) en terre cuite vintage sur Etsy ou Amazon, en variant tailles, formes et hauteurs, puis plantez-y les fleurs mentionnées ci-dessus. Comme dans un vrai jardin, regroupez vos pots pour obtenir un effet cottage.

N’ayant pas accès à la nappe phréatique d’un jardin classique, les plantations en pot nécessitent un arrosage attentif. Surveillez la météo et, par temps chaud, arrosez généreusement.

Article initialement publié dans [*AD* Moyen-Orient](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/recreate-the-english-cottage-garden-look-from-taylor-swift-travis-kelce-engagement-photos).

title: The Best Hotels in Norway for a Scenic Stay Among the Fjords and Mountains
content:
The best hotels in [Norway](https://www.admiddleeast.com/gallery/best-places-to-visit-in-2025-according-to-ad-editors){: target="_blank"} don’t just frame the scenery – they’re part of the story. Each one reflects a different side of the country, from historic fishing warehouses and 19th-century hideaways to hillside cabins steeped in *friluftsliv*: the Norwegian love of open-air living.



[#image: /photos/68c3dccd7707303d2921061b]

There are some rather wonderful things about Norway that might be useful to know before I begin this piece. It has, for example, been voted the happiest country in the world; it is also credited with the invention of skiing and has the longest coastline in Europe. But it is, perhaps, best known for its outstanding landscapes, the deep blue fjords created during the Ice Age, mountains with cascading waterfalls and the perfect rust coloured cabins that sit along the rugged coastline.

## Also Read: [Northern Lights: The 10 Best Places to See the Magical Aurora Borealis](https://www.admiddleeast.com/gallery/northern-lights-best-places-to-see-the-magical-aurora-borealis)


[#image: /photos/68c2cd6136ccf055d63c3bb3]

There are over 1,700 fjords in Norway, and it is on the West Coast where some of the most beautiful and dramatic landscapes can be found. This is primarily why 62 Nord has chosen this part of Norway to create three extraordinary hotels. The first of these hotels is in Alesund, reached from the airport via an extraordinary deep-sea tunnel. The town has a long history as a fishing port, but there is also a fascinating architectural twist to Alesund. After a fire devastated nearly half of the town (many of the buildings were wooden and the fire spread quickly), it was rebuilt in 1904. The craftspeople drafted in to rebuild it had studied abroad and were influenced by [the Art Nouveau style](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/discover-iconic-art-nouveau-architecture-in-vienna-walking-tour-guide-for-design-lovers){: target="_blank"}, which they combined with motifs from the Viking Age and Norse mythology.



[#externallink: /externallinks/650303f6bb917946d989f4c0]



[#image: /photos/68c2cdadfa4c7768c3149e17]

## The Hotel Brosundet


[#image: /photos/68c2cdf036ccf055d63c3bb5]

+++button-group

[Book Now](https://cna.st/affiliate-link/UWxN5ZEu9Mcqjud7m3NKrqQiPk2F5fdBvejmRsDAQB42W4bSNTSsZmZKyxNidfALj3BvrJRnHsbueeEJ5CiXJXPM957RbYiHBq8jVez7aRHxJFKaqJY73QRGzAuFqR4XieR1qzWNuBobjnRAvCurrv "Book Now"){: rel="sponsored" target="_blank" isAffiliateLink="true"}

+++

The Hotel Brosundet in Alesund was once a warehouse for local fisheries, and was one of the few wooden buildings that did not burn down. A warehouse that once stored fish might not seem like an inspired idea for a hotel location, but it is actually everything that you could possibly hope for if you were to conjure up an image of chic Nordic hospitality. The large reception area has an open [fireplace](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/a-faux-fireplace-brings-warmth-and-cosiness-to-this-minimal-living-room-in-dubai){: target="_blank"} big enough to host a large gathering of Vikings, with a chimney flue rising dramatically through the centre of the building. A fire is exactly what should be at the heart of a Norwegian hotel, where the cold is a constant for many months of the year. There are huge chunky beams that support the lower floors, instantly making the hotel feel solid and grounded in its heritage. Tucked into the corner of the reception is a small but beautifully curated shop selling handmade sweaters in creams, greys and taupe by the brand O.A.



[#image: /photos/68c2ce2636ccf055d63c3bb7]

Rooms are light and airy, filled with carefully chosen furniture, with windows looking out over the iconic Brosundet channel at one end and Art Nouveau architecture at the other. On the lower-ground floor is a beautifully designed gym and wellness area, including a large outdoor balcony with carved stone baths filled with hot water and bubbles, the perfect remedy after a dip in the Harbour, which can be accessed by a wooden jetty. In the adjoining building to the hotel is a beautiful deli called Butikken with a selection of Norwegian delicacies all beautifully displayed on wide wooden shelves. There are also rooms for wine tastings and private dinners.



[#image: /photos/68c2ce7836ccf055d63c3bb9]

Navigating the West Coast is easiest with a boat on hand, and this is all part of the service at 62 Nord. There really isn’t a more beautiful way to travel between hotels, a cosy rug on the lap, and a country revealing itself as you glide past. The rust coloured wooden summer houses clustered on rocky peninsulas, rugged mountains rising straight up from deep fjord waters.

## Union Øye Hotel


[#image: /photos/68c3de12d02ce634a26f900d]

+++button-group

[Book Now](https://cna.st/affiliate-link/2kPDMaDggM5J7QcMdmQpCe23AZw8Qbrm9LAPvrmBLViJRSUkLm9R51onSuVv4hhYJw8ftE5tCLV9urAeZRY3kG12n9GXyv8HWsJ9f33hCpLUYsjjP1fJFVBQg2 "Book Now"){: rel="sponsored" target="_blank" isAffiliateLink="true"}

+++

Waterfalls tumbling over grey rock as the snow melts, and the wild beauty of Norway revealing itself through every window. In a secluded hamlet at the end of the fjord surrounded by the craggy wild mountains, the Union Øye Hotel appears, a turreted, pale pink [fairytale](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/best-fairytale-castles-in-germany-that-seem-right-out-of-a-disney-movie){: target="_blank"} house. It has been here since 1891, a refuge for tweed-clad mountaineers who came to Norway to practice for expeditions to the Himalayas. Each of the bedrooms is named after the writers, mountaineers, artists and members of the Royal family who have stayed here. From the suit of armour that greets you in reception, to the cosy library filled with first edition leather-bound books, the Union Øye feels like the country home of a very well-read Norwegian aristocrat. Parked outside the hotel is a Morgan; guests can hire the car for a scenic drive through the mountains, with a picnic hamper packed for lunch.



[#image: /photos/68c3de7c9cdbfc06554a2280]



[#image: /photos/68c3de917707303d2921061d]

The hotel has been exquisitely decorated, the interiors a mix of wood panelled walls, dusty pink velvet sofas, and bedrooms wallpapered in the most perfect floral patterns. The colour palette is a blend of sage green, mushroom and vintage raspberry pink. There are tapestry rugs and cushions with fringed brocade – but there is nothing ‘fussy’ or contrived about this hotel. The large and light-filled conservatory, [painted in a beautiful sage green](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/green-room-decor-ideas-how-to-use-green-in-interior-design){: target="_blank"}, is a new addition where meals are served. Even in here, the cane chairs, oak leaf metal chandeliers and cleverly designed wooden chequered floor sit harmoniously with the rest of the building. Surrounding this jewel box of a hotel are mountains; some of the biggest and most challenging hiking paths in the country are found here. There is also e-biking, which is an excellent way to explore some of the countryside if you don't fancy a vertical ascent but a rather more relaxed road trip. Union Øye also has one of the most beautiful saunas you will ever visit. At the end of a long wooden jetty, there is a charcoal black house with a huge glass window overlooking the snow-capped mountains. Birds dive into the water, catching fish, and the waterfalls tumble through the Nordic pines, and the silence isn’t deafening; it is magnificent.

## Storfjord


[#image: /photos/68c3dd839cdbfc06554a227e]

+++button-group

[Book Now](https://cna.st/affiliate-link/3Hvxm9czoAnBvsqu6eN8yzhCpJRg8pXux565DubbLEyoLua2jocRppAFGp5sM5a71C2QCfVLk4zAMDE7mpp4YcFg4Vn8VyNWhmyanMWjnRFi6v6fo5xAjkabXEZN84PtubscUZ7rgbv2LSyTwcU6MjKro6FgyaNrNCpwy7g "Book Now"){: rel="sponsored" target="_blank" isAffiliateLink="true"}

+++

The third hotel in the trio of the best hotels in Norway is Storfjord, situated in the remote hamlet of Glomset, and the journey here is by car and electric ferry; again, the views are so spectacular that the drive is over too quickly. At Storfjord, the mood changes again; surrounded by trees, the smell is of freshly cut timber, pine needles and damp earth. There is a word in Norwegian, ‘Friluftsliv’, which translates as open-air living, first popularised by the playwright Henrik Ibsen. This hotel, like a very glamorous Winter cabin, with its partly turfed roof, has outstanding views over the fjords below. There is a cosy dining room, with another roaring fire and a sitting room decorated with traditional painted wooden bowls, and lined with more books. The libraries in each of the hotels are exceptional and are a complete passion of the owners. There are sofas upholstered in just the right shades of linen, with armchairs positioned everywhere to just sit and quietly read. The rooms, with underfloor heating, are decorated simply and beautifully, a peaceful, cosy retreat after a hike or a dip in the hot tub.



[#image: /photos/68c3ddc5d02ce634a26f900b]

This wild and wonderful coastline is one of the most spectacular locations in the world. The hotels that 62 Nord has created achieve the almost impossible. Each of the hotels sits in a landscape of unrivalled natural beauty, and yet each rests quietly in its position, as if it had always been there, waiting for guests to discover the freedom of ‘*Filuftsliv*’.
