title: Le milliardaire qui a épousé Brigitte Bardot et lancé 10 000 roses sur La Madrague en hélicoptère
content:
## Gunter Sachs, un jet-setteur excentrique et héritier d'une grande fortune
*« Play-boy milliardaire »*, *« extravagant »*… [Gunter Sachs](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/saint-tropez-villas-mythiques-fortunes) est décrit au cours de sa vie comme un jet-senteur (très) riche qui court après les femmes et n’a pas peur d’utiliser les grands moyens pour leur faire savoir.



[#image: /photos/695261ded9dbcd1cfbb63ebd]|||Le jeune Gunter Sachs avec ses amis et sa famille sur un bateau à moteur à Saint-Tropez, en France.|||

### Les mots de Brigitte Bardot rapportés par Ardisson
Par exemple, juste après avoir épousé [Brigitte Bardot](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/brigitte-bardot-bastide-provencale-cannes) à Las Vegas, le photographe et collectionneur décide de monter à bord de son hélicoptère et de lancer sur [La Madrague](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/brigitte-bardot-maisons-deces), propriété tropézienne de *BB*, une myriade de roses. Sur le plateau de l’émission de [Thierry Ardisson](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/thierry-ardisson-appartement-rue-de-rivoli) archivé par l’INA, *Gunter Sachs : sa vie avec Bardot et l'astrologie chez Thierry Ardisson*, on apprend que Brigitte Bardot disait de son ancien époux : *« Il me fallait un caractère à la Gunter Sachs, qui ne s’est pas laissé faire »* tout comme *« c’est l’un des seuls moments de ma vie où j’ai réellement été impressionnée et éblouie »*. Grand collectionneur d’art, ami [d’Andy Warhol,](https://www.admagazine.fr/adinspiration/article/andy-warhol-retour-sur-vie-oeuvre-pape-pop-art) dont les œuvres décorent allègrement ses maisons autour du monde, Gunter Sachs est né le 14 novembre 1932 à Mainberg près de Schweinfurt en Allemagne, décédé le 7 mai 2011 à Gstaad.

+++slideshow

[#image: /photos/69526213631ad33a59b818db]|||L'actrice française Brigitte Bardot et son nouveau mari, l'industriel allemand Gunther Sachs, quittent l'aéroport international de Los Angeles à bord d'un vol à destination de Papeete, à Tahiti, le 16 juillet, pour un séjour de deux semaines.|||

[#image: /photos/695262216ec0cad89f25eb18]|||14 juillet 1966 - Van Nuys, Californie. Une rose à la main, la star de cinéma française Brigitte Bardot et son nouveau mari, l'industriel allemand Gunther Sachs Von Opel, descendent de leur avion après un vol en provenance de Las Vegas, où le couple s'est marié le 13 juillet lors d'une cérémonie surprise. Les jeunes mariés ont insisté pour passer leur lune de miel au Mexique, mais selon certaines informations contradictoires, ils auraient passé leur idylle post-mariage à Tahiti.|||

[#image: /photos/6952622d130b69ac641bdbfc]|||Brigitte Bardot avec Gunther Sachs à Saint-Tropez, France, en août 1966.|||

[#image: /photos/695262396ec0cad89f25eb1a]|||Brigitte Bardot avec Gunther Sachs à Saint-Tropez, France, en août 1966.|||

[#image: /photos/6952624fd9dbcd1cfbb63ebf]|||Brigitte Bardot avec Gunther Sachs à Saint-Tropez, France, en août 1966.|||

+++

### La rencontre entre Gunter Sachs et Brigitte Bardot, un coup de foudre
La fortune de Gunter Sachs est familiale avant tout. Ses deux grands-pères sont de renommés industriels allemands. Son grand-père paternel Ernst Sachs est l’un des fondateurs de l’empire automobile Fichtel & Sachs, tandis que son grand-père maternel est Wilhelm von Opel, fils d'Adam Opel, le fondateur d'Opel. L’histoire d’amour entre Gunther Sachs et [Brigitte Bardot](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/hommage-brigitte-bardot-maisons-iconiques) débute à l’été 1966 lorsque la jeune femme se rend dans un restaurant à Gassin, près de [Saint-Tropez](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/saint-tropez-villa-mas-provencal), où est attablé le milliardaire. Coup de foudre. Le dîner est suivi d’une soirée endiablée au Papagayo, célèbre boîte de nuit tropézienne, puis d’une chambre à l’hôtel La Ponche — entièrement repensé il y a quelques années par l’architecte [Fabrizio Casiraghi](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/villa-miami-fabrizio-casiraghi). L’idylle se concrétise par une demande en mariage des plus rapides : le couple s’envole vers Las Vegas pour se marier. Une cérémonie qui aurait duré en tout et pour tout huit minutes, le 14 juillet 1966.



[#image: /photos/6952627b6ec0cad89f25eb1c]|||Gunter Sachs, époux de l'actrice française Brigitte Bardot, lors d'une conférence de presse le 8 juin, où il a inauguré une collection d'art moderne à la Villa Stuck.|||

### 10 000 roses lancées sur La Madrague de Bardot
À leur retour à Saint-Tropez, Gunter Sachs monte à bord de son hélicoptère pour jeter 10 000 roses rouges sur la villa de [Brigitte Bardot](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/brigitte-bardot-appartement-paris-deces) en symbole de son amour éternel. Si la jeune femme refuse de s’installer dans l’appartement parisien de son nouveau mari, elle accepte de visiter sa belle-famille en Allemagne. Le couple vit séparément et bat de l’aile lorsque Bardot rencontre [Serge Gainsbourg](https://www.admagazine.fr/galerie/serge-gainsbourg-maison-musee) avec qui elle écrit plusieurs chansons. On lui attribue d’ailleurs une liaison avec le chanteur et Gunter Sachs exige que l’album enregistré ne sorte pas — il le sera seulement en 1986. Chacun entretiendrait de son côté des relations extra-conjugales, et la sortie du couple lors d’un dîner à l’Élysée en décembre 1967 est la dernière. Ils divorcent officiellement deux ans plus tard, en 1969.

+++slideshow

[#image: /photos/695262b56ec0cad89f25eb1e]|||La famille Sachs s'amuse lorsque le père, Gunter, plonge dans la piscine de leur villa à Saint-Tropez.|||

[#image: /photos/695262c44bdd9534e763a540]|||Gunter Sachs avec son épouse Mirja et leurs fils Christian Gunnar et Claus Alexander (à droite) à Saint-Tropez, jouant au frisbee, France, 2004.|||

[#image: /photos/695263ec130b69ac641bdbfe]|||Vue depuis la propriété de Gunter Sachs à Saint-Tropez, France, 2004.|||

[#image: /photos/69526400631ad33a59b818dd]|||Vue depuis la propriété de Gunter Sachs à Saint-Tropez, France, 2004.|||

[#image: /photos/6952642a631ad33a59b818df]|||La propriété de Gunter Sachs à Saint-Tropez, France, 2004.|||

[#image: /photos/6952644c631ad33a59b818e1]|||La famille Sachs s'amuse lorsque le père, Gunter, plonge dans la piscine de leur villa à Saint-Tropez.|||

+++

### Fin de vie de Gunter Sachs, diagnostiqué Alzheimer
Après son divorce, Gunter Sachspouse le mannequin Mirja Larsson avec qui il partage sa vie au gré de ses différentes propriétés — de son opulente villa sur les hauteurs de Saint-Tropez à son chalet à Gstaad. Le [milliardaire](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/grandes-fortunes-mondiales-interieurs-extravagants) a 78 ans quand il apprend être atteint de la maladie d’Alzheimer et décide de mettre fin à ses jours. Il laisse un mot expliquant son geste : *« La mort est la seule issue. Pourtant, ma capacité à raisonner logiquement n’est pas (encore) affectée. Je peux toujours penser, réfléchir. En revanche, ma mémoire se dégrade à vitesse grand V. Je suis de plus en plus souvent distrait. Et, en dépit de mon niveau d’éducation élevé, ma capacité à m’exprimer dans toutes les langues que je parle couramment est très altérée, ce qui crée des décalages dans ma conversation. Cette menace sur mon intégrité intellectuelle est un critère absolu pour mettre un terme à ma vie. »*

+++slideshow

[#image: /photos/695264bfd9dbcd1cfbb63ec1]|||Gunter Sachs se détend et pose sur un canapé, dans les années 2000.|||

[#image: /photos/695264d36ec0cad89f25eb20]|||Chambre confortable de Gunter Sachs, années 2000.|||

[#image: /photos/6952651d130b69ac641bdc00]|||Salon de la propriété de Gunter Sachs à Saint-Tropez, France, 2004.|||

[#image: /photos/695266506ec0cad89f25eb22]|||Mirja, l'épouse de Gunter Sachs, et leur fils Claus Alexander en pleine conversation à Saint-Tropez, France, 2004.|||

+++

+++slideshow

[#image: /photos/695267146ec0cad89f25eb24]|||Gunter Sachs posant sur un canapé, dans les années 2000.|||

[#image: /photos/6952678a631ad33a59b818e3]|||Gunter Sachs - Intérieur de l'une de ses nombreuses maisons, années 2000.|||

[#image: /photos/695267284bdd9534e763a542]|||L'œuvre « Marilyn Monroe » d'Andy Warhol chez Gunter Sachs.|||

[#image: /photos/6952673f4bdd9534e763a544]|||Gunter Sachs - Intérieur de l'une de ses nombreuses maisons, années 2000.|||

[#image: /photos/6952675c4bdd9534e763a546]|||Salon de la demeure de Gunter Sachs, Suisse, 2005.|||

[#image: /photos/69526768d9dbcd1cfbb63ec4]|||Mirja, l'épouse de Gunter Sachs, lisant dans un fauteuil, dans les années 2000.|||

[#image: /photos/69526774130b69ac641bdc02]|||Gunter Sachs avec son épouse Mirja dans leur salon, dans les années 2000.|||

[#image: /photos/695267a2d9dbcd1cfbb63ec6]|||Gunter Sachs - Intérieur de l'une de ses nombreuses maisons, années 2000.|||

[#image: /photos/695267b2d9dbcd1cfbb63ec8]|||Salle de billard dans la demeure de Gunter Sachs, Suisse, 2005.|||

+++



[#image: /photos/695267c3631ad33a59b818e5]|||Gunter Sachs - Intérieur de l'une de ses nombreuses maisons, années 2000.|||

title: Step Inside a Desert Home Carved On Top of an Ancient Black Lava Field in Utah's Mojave Desert
content:
As inspiration for a decorative scheme, the Utah landscape is a gift. When Design Studio JAC was asked to reimagine the interior of this [contemporary desert home](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/contemporary-desert-home-ideas-nevada){: target="_blank"}, the US state’s rocky terrain provided everything it needed to create a restorative sanctuary in the wilderness. Jourdan Hammond, who founded the Miami- and Belgium-based studio alongside Ashley Viola and Céline Hellin, led the project.



[#image: /photos/6964a7c93cef600c933b0675]



[#image: /photos/6964a7b13cef600c933b0667]

​Sitting atop an ancient black lava field within the Mojave Desert’s northern reach, the family holiday home’s striking black façade is a mirror to the surrounding volcanic rock – hence its name, Rock House. On the horizon are the vivid copper-toned sandstone formations of the Red Cliffs Desert Reserve, with the Pine Valley Mountains rising in the distance. “The landscape feels both rugged and tranquil – a meeting of black basalt rock, desert flora and fauna, and ever-shifting light,” says Hammond. “Our goal was to create a home that feels as though it’s [always belonged here](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/this-spectacular-beach-home-in-abu-dhabi-was-inspired-by-the-colours-of-the-sun-and-the-sea){: target="_blank"}.”



[#image: /photos/6964a798df22aa5935268e3a]



[#image: /photos/6964a798df22aa5935268e3b]

Originally built in 2014, the two-storey property had been gutted by fire and its interiors completely cleared when the current owners acquired it. “That blank canvas gave us incredible creative freedom,” Hammond recalls. “We could rethink every surface, every proportion, every detail.” The homeowners view this place as an escape (“a place to slow down, connect with the landscape, and find inspiration”), so [the designers](https://www.designstudiojac.com/){: target="_blank"} wanted every room to have a connection to the outdoors – “whether that’s through a framed view, a material texture, or the way the light moves across surfaces”.



[#image: /photos/6964a7a7df22aa5935268e40]

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/6964a7a2864fb03e540d3c61]

[#image: /photos/6964a7983cef600c933b0661]

+++

With its flat rooflines, thick [stucco](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/stucco-ideas-for-modern-homes){: target="_blank"} walls and earthy palette, the house is typical of modern desert architecture; in response, Hammond’s design feels grounded and contemplative. “The views transform with the light: glowing in warm tones at dawn, stark and sculptural at midday, and finally awash in oranges as dusk settles – the desert home’s colour story was conceived to echo those transitions,” he says.



[#image: /photos/6964a7a8df22aa5935268e41]

Colour is used sparingly but with intention, the palette dominated by warm neutrals with occasional accents of rust, olive and black. Tactile organic materials bring their own gentle hues, as well as a sense of durability. Lime-plastered walls diffuse a soft glow, while wide-plank Belgian wood floors mirror the dark volcanic stone outside, rooting the home in its setting. In the open-plan living area, a cold-rolled steel fireplace rises from floor to ceiling, echoing a staircase in the same material; both were crafted by a local artisan.​



[#image: /photos/6964a7a0864fb03e540d3c5e]

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/6964a7a1f31dce4f300e726b]

[#image: /photos/6964a7b9df22aa5935268e48]

+++

Throughout the house, stone islands, honed counters and sculptural millwork read almost like geological formations, [carved rather than constructed](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/desert-rock-a-new-cave-like-saudi-resort-was-carved-out-of-the-hejaz-mountains-eco-tourism-middle-east){: target="_blank"}. Bedrooms are almost cave-like in their enveloping calm. “The walls are wrapped entirely in microcement, creating a meditative atmosphere,” adds Hammond. The effect is one of quiet luxury, where craftsmanship replaces ornamentation and expansive windows frame the wilderness like living artworks.​

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/6964a7a93cef600c933b0664]

[#image: /photos/6964a7a93cef600c933b0665]

+++

Design Studio JAC created many custom pieces for the home, often in warm greys that complement the landscape. Among these are an oversized sofa; charred-wood coffee tables designed by Céline Hellin; wooden stools in the monolithic kitchen; and custom beds with integrated timber elements, such as nightstands and desks. These bespoke features were combined with minimalist pieces by Modernist and contemporary designers: vintage Tobia Scarpa chairs in the breakfast nook, a pair of charcoal Utrecht chairs by Gerrit Rietveld for [Cassina](https://www.cassina.com/gb/en/products/utrecht-armchair.html?cas_rivestimento=L-L010#637-utrecht-armchair_53619){: target="_blank"} beside the fireplace, and a boulder-like [microcement](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/curved-interiors-and-micro-cement-walls-make-this-bengaluru-home-a-masterclass-in-warm-minimalism){: target="_blank"} dining table by [Armand & Francine](https://armandfrancine.be/collectie.html){: target="_blank"}, teamed with an [Apparatus Studio](https://apparatusstudio.com/products/pip-arrow?variant=42465563279423){: target="_blank"} chandelier.

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/6964a7a7864fb03e540d3c63]

[#image: /photos/6964a7aedf22aa5935268e43]

+++

​Desert breezes blow freely through the terraces and [courtyards](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/contemporary-courtyard-home-in-abu-dhabi-reinterprets-traditional-arabic-style-modern-design){: target="_blank"}, reflecting the designers’ meticulous use of everything the landscape has to offer. “During each site visit, we photo-documented the cacti, desert plants and rock formations, building a palette drawn directly from the environment,” explains Hammond. “We also collected stones and brought them back to our studio to colour-match the exterior stucco – finding the perfect tones so the house would appear to emerge from the lava field itself.”



[#image: /photos/6964a7be3cef600c933b066f]

Even the finishing flourishes were an [homage to the desert](https://www.admiddleeast.com/gallery/7-dazzling-desert-homes){: target="_blank"}. “At the end of every project, we add florals and greenery to bring the space to life. In this case, we foraged the area for naturally dried branches and grasses, which quickly turned into a bit of a comedy – we were hiking and climbing over boulders in search of the perfect sculptural pieces,” remembers Hammond. “Later, when a landscaping crew came to tidy the grounds, we couldn’t resist digging through their pile of trimmings for more treasures. They looked completely puzzled – it was a true ‘one person’s trash is another’s treasure’ moment.”



[#image: /photos/6964a7c03cef600c933b0670]

In spite of the project’s painstaking attention to detail, it feels effortless – and in one sense at least, it was. “Design happens in different ways – sometimes it’s a slow unfolding, sometimes it comes in a single moment of clarity,” muses Hammond. “With this home, the vision revealed itself immediately. On the flight back to Miami after our first site visit, we sketched the entire design, and remarkably, the concept remained nearly unchanged. It felt less like invention and more like discovery – as if the home was already there, waiting to be realised.” [*@designstudiojac*](https://www.instagram.com/designstudiojac){: target="_blank"}



[#image: /photos/6964a8a3f31dce4f300e7275]

title: Estilo mediterráneo: Una casa utiliza materiales sostenibles para renovarse
content:
El **estilo mediterráneo** está presente en cada uno de los detalles que hacen de esta casa un lugar único, pensado para relajarse sin prisas y disfrutar de la armonía entre la luz natural, los materiales nobles y la serenidad que envuelve cada espacio.

En Selvapuram, Coimbatore, donde las casas familiares se asoman a campos abiertos salpicados de altos árboles, **una residencia de 147 metros cuadrados rinde homenaje a la sencillez y la vida tranquila** de nuestros antepasados. Diseñada por el estudio de sostenibilidad Bhutha Earthen Architecture para el profesional de TI Vijayanadh y su esposa Mahalakshmi, esta casa reinventa la arquitectura tradicional tamil desde una perspectiva mediterránea y contemporánea, priorizando la sostenibilidad sin sacrificar la comodidad.



[#image: /photos/69656cb5d11e939d8734dd68]|||Una alfombra de yute y algodón de la India oriental sirve como decoración.|||

## Materiales sostenibles y una construcción tradicional
“No solo lo imaginamos para nosotros, sino para las generaciones venideras”, explicó la pareja. Para Mahalakshmi, quien creció en una casa ancestral, el proyecto fue profundamente personal: **una oportunidad de recrear un hogar que evoque el estilo mediterráneo, rodeado de naturaleza,** que celebra su herencia y al mismo tiempo recupera su individualidad. “Meticulosamente integrado en su entorno natural, cada aspecto del diseño refleja un profundo aprecio por las técnicas de construcción tradicionales”, explican los arquitectos principales Vinoth Kumar y Srinath Gowtham. **La gama de materiales sostenibles de la residencia refleja este compromiso.**



[#image: /photos/69656cb5d11e939d8734dd69]|||Este ventanal multifuncional también funciona como rincón de descanso, diseñado con un alféizar extendido con acabado en piedra verde tradicional india. Estantes laterales enmarcan el ventanal en ambos lados, transformando el nicho en una elegante exposición.|||

La casa de Coimbatore se abre con un porche de entrada tradicional que presenta un thinnai con pilares de ladrillo visto, un gesto inmediato hacia la vida comunitaria y rural. En el interior, la planta baja se despliega con una fluidez intuitiva que hace guiño al **estilo mediterráneo**. La sala, conectada visualmente con la cocina y la escalera, está presidida por un [amplio ventanal](https://www.admagazine.com/articulos/un-departamento-moderno-aumenta-su-espacio-con-ventanales-de-doble-altura){: target="_blank"} que enmarca el paisaje. **El telón de fondo siempre presente es la naturaleza** y las ondulantes colinas que cambian a medida que avanza el día. “La alcoba se convirtió en la favorita de la familia, donde se lee o se disfruta de una siesta”, explican los arquitectos.



[#image: /photos/69656cb5d11e939d8734dd6a]

El ladrillo visto envuelve los espacios habitables, acentuado por dinteles de piedra, detalles de madera y ménsulas metálicas, todo ello coronado por tejados inclinados de terracota. Estos materiales fundacionales se extienden a los interiores, donde las baldosas de terracota cubren los suelos junto con la piedra Kota, creando una sutil variación de texturas bajo los pies. La historia del color es igualmente considerada: marrón barro, blanco chuna, verde lima, marrón leñoso y gris humo crean un esquema que parece a la vez sobrio y rico, asentando la casa en su paisaje a la vez que mantiene la calidez visual.



[#image: /photos/69656cb5d11e939d8734dd6b]|||La cocina cuenta con cerramientos minimalistas y una distribución abierta, adaptada al ritmo de trabajo de las amas de casa. Las paredes están revestidas con yeso liso de cal verde, mezclado a mano y aplicado con técnicas tradicionales|||



[#image: /photos/69656cb5d11e939d8734dd6c]|||Un armario compacto de madera antigua ofrece un estante funcional en la zona de servicio, y su carácter desgastado se suaviza con las plantas de interior en cascada.|||

En la planta superior se encuentra el [dormitorio de los niños](https://www.admagazine.com/articulos/recamara-infantil-como-disenar-habitaciones-para-sonar-y-jugar){: target="_blank"} y una sala multiusos diseñada para adaptarse a diversas actividades: práctica musical, trabajo y contemplación. Los **altos ventanales enmarcan las vistas de la terraza abierta** que se transforma a lo largo del día de plataforma de yoga matutina a comedor vespertino, ofreciendo siempre una conexión con el cielo y el atardecer. Aquí, la arquitectura cumple su función esencial: crear un espacio flexible que se adapta a los ritmos de la vida cotidiana.

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/69656cb5d11e939d8734dd6d]|||Las camas bajas anclan esta habitación con proporciones sobrias que se adaptan a las preferencias de sus habitantes.|||

[#image: /photos/69656cb5d11e939d8734dd6e]

+++

**Cada detalle refleja una profunda reverencia por la materialidad y la artesanía.** Incluso los canalones de acero dulce para el agua de lluvia, en forma de canales rectangulares que dirigen el agua hacia pozos de recogida, equilibran la utilidad con la expresión arquitectónica. Las [puertas de madera](https://www.admagazine.com/articulos/puertas-para-closet-11-disenos-para-un-armario-unico-y-moderno){: target="_blank"} artesanales presentan patrones ondulados, y sus tiradores, elaborados con combinaciones de madera y piedra, resultan robustos al tacto. Diseñada con un enfoque en la tradición y una atención a la sostenibilidad, la casa es un testimonio de que la sobriedad, el arraigo y el respeto por la tierra pueden crear espacios de profundo confort para las generaciones venideras.



[#image: /photos/69656cb5d11e939d8734dd6f]|||Las paredes de ladrillo visto se combinan con dinteles de piedra natural, detalles de madera y ménsulas metálicas bajo un tejado inclinado de terracota, mientras que las rocas naturales y los zócalos de piedra protegen la estructura contra la humedad y las termitas.|||



[#image: /photos/69656cb5d11e939d8734dd70]

*Artículo publicado originalmente en* **AD *India*.**

title: This Australian Country Home’s Décor Is Inspired by Christian Dior’s Favourite Shade of Grey
content:
Which colour could be more apt for a couple who live between the city and the country than a particular shade of Christian Dior grey?

Victoria’s Macedon Ranges is one of [Australia’s most picturesque regions](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/this-modern-house-in-sydney-was-designed-to-be-at-one-with-its-scenic-clifftop-location-nina-maya){: target="_blank"}, famous for landmarks such as Hanging Rock and the Black Forest. On the latter’s edge sits Woodend: in the 19^th^\-century, it was a key stopping point for gold prospectors and is scattered with traditional homesteads. There were also boarding houses, which is how this particular country home started life – as a hodgepodge of rooms and apartments, rich in historical detail but somewhat disjointed. Australian interior designer David Hicks has rescued the building from its chequered past, transforming it into a timeless rural retreat inspired by global art and design.



[#image: /photos/6971d98235648be64b93d6a2]

The home was created for a couple who divide their time between the country and the city. “They were existing clients – we had already designed their city penthouse, which is very sophisticated and elegant,” [explains Hicks.](https://davidhicks.com/){: target="_blank"} “For this home, they wanted something more relaxed — a melting pot of collectibles from their travels. The brief was to create a refined, eclectic design that could also serve as a backdrop for their diverse art collection. It needed to embrace the spirit of the Australian countryside while incorporating European influences, blending the past and present in a way that felt authentic and modern.”



[#image: /photos/6971d98135648be64b93d6a1]

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/6971d981740fccc8ac34c90c]

[#image: /photos/6971d95c35648be64b93d69e]

+++

Hicks wanted to preserve the building’s architectural integrity, so he kept many period details, including the [Arts & Crafts](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/arts-and-crafts-movement-in-this-reinvented-1900s-melbourne-home){: target="_blank"} fireplace in the living room and the stained-glass front door, which was relocated to the side of the home so the entrance could be replaced with a set of French windows (these bring light into the corridor that now forms the “spine” of the house, dividing the living areas and guest suite from the study, library and principal suite). “Wall panelling, architectural details, and even creaky floorboards and uneven [ceilings](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/ceiling-decoration-is-making-a-comeback-plus-11-ideas-to-elevate-your-fifth-wall){: target="_blank"} were retained or sympathetically replicated to honour the home’s layered past and celebrate the charm that comes with imperfection,” adds Hicks, who introduced contemporary grid panelling for “depth and rhythm” and installed porcelain travertine flooring for “durability and a sense of understated luxury”.

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/6971d9ac35648be64b93d6a5]

[#image: /photos/6971d963a0bea86900d6f6ef]

+++



[#image: /photos/6971d961a0bea86900d6f6ee]

With this improved layout and timeless backdrop in place, the characterful decor could take shape. Hicks took his cue from the clients’ art collection, which spans antique, Asian, and contemporary works. “Each room was composed as a curated tableau, blending decorative minimalism with eclectic references and enabling a subtle conversation between pieces,” he says. “Artworks anchor the design and influenced the layering of materials and furnishings.” Thus, a warm grey shade inspired by [Christian Dior’s](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/kim-jones-dior-spring-summer-2024-menswear-show-set-design){: target="_blank"} Paris atelier was chosen for the walls, offset with cream on ceilings and woodwork to give a neutral, sophisticated base. Accent hues are borrowed from paintings – yellows in the living room are echoed on custom sofas upholstered in [Dedar](https://dedar.com/){: target="_blank"} velvet, while green tones distinguish the kitchen decor, where sage-painted cabinets mingle with emerald-green marble. “These accents animate the interior without overwhelming it,” says Hicks.



[#image: /photos/6971d970a0bea86900d6f6f1]



[#image: /photos/6971d981740fccc8ac34c90d]

The designer produced many custom pieces for the space, in luxe materials that suggest a sense of continuity with the country home’s heritage. These include a walnut-and-chrome dining table, walnut bedside tables (“to add weight and warmth”), and silk rugs for essential softness (complemented by cushions in silks from [Rubelli](https://www.rubelli.com/en/){: target="_blank"} and Dedar). “These bespoke elements were chosen to sit harmoniously with antiques, [midcentury](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/this-midcentury-apartment-in-cairo-deftly-mixes-old-and-new-with-nods-to-traditional-egyptian-crafts){: target="_blank"} and Asian furniture,” says Hicks, whose vintage finds include the Javanese wicker chairs in the living room and guest suite, a selection of oriental lacquer cabinets, and a Louis Poulsen pendant over the dining table.



[#image: /photos/6971d9a7740fccc8ac34c911]

Once a fleeting stop for travellers, the home has now swapped transience for permanence, providing its residents with lasting joy. Its new [corrugated-iron roof](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/this-desert-home-with-a-tent-like-copper-canopy-feels-like-a-luxurious-tent){: target="_blank"} – a staple of Australian homesteads – is there for the long haul, holding out the promise of evenings spent lounging on the shaded verandas beneath. “It sums up my design philosophy, which is about building interiors that feel both lived-in and considered,” says Hicks. “Every object has presence and purpose.”



[#image: /photos/6971d9aaa0bea86900d6f6f5]

title: Art Basel Qatar 2026: What Not To Miss in Doha
content:
After months of anticipation, [Art Basel Qatar](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/art-basel-qatar-middle-east-2026-art-fair-dohaheres-everything-to-know){: target="_blank"} has finally opened its doors in the capital of Doha, planting the iconic Swiss-born art fair in the region. It marks a significant milestone in the city, as well as the wider Gulf states’ burgeoning art scene that is fast-attracting worldwide attention.



[#image: /photos/698317d22023714cf83c8a79]

On VIP opening day, which took place on February 3, positive vibes and fresh energy was high amongst fairgoers – collectors, journalists, art professionals – roaming and socialising in the fair’s halls in Doha’s upscale [Msheireb Downtown Doha area](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/doha-design-district-msheireb-downtown-qatar-vibrant-creative-community){: target="_blank"}. Until February 7, the fair will be showcasing 87 international exhibitors and 84 artists, many of whom hail from the MENASA region.

Under the title of *Becoming*, the fair was designed through the eyes of its [artistic director](https://www.instagram.com/wael_shawky/?hl=en){: target="_blank"}, Wael Shawky, who is himself an acclaimed multidisciplinary artist and educator from Egypt. Having Shawky in such an important position has been praised by many, indicating a particular focus on artists – their narratives and practices – rather than the commercial aspect of the fair.



[#image: /photos/698317d012d70f6cb68130dc]

“I didn’t want it to be a regular art fair that is only considered a marketplace,” Shawky told *AD.* “I was involved in everything – the choice of artworks and artists. I wanted the first edition of Art Basel Qatar to be more like a statement. In the end, it’s about how to present the first edition in the Gulf, not only as a commercial place, but also as a place for quality. To make this first edition unique and not like any art fair that people have seen before was really part of the mission.”

Another unique factor of Art Basel Qatar is its solo-artist presentations, as each gallery has dedicated its booth to the work of a single artist. [Shawky, who is known for his storytelling film artworks](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/egyptian-artist-wael-shawky-exhibition-at-luma-arles-blends-of-mysticism-and-history){: target="_blank"}, hopes that the gallery presentations, diving into figuration, abstraction, history, socio-politics and collective memory, among other issues, will produce intellectual discourses.



[#image: /photos/698317d22023714cf83c8a7a]

“When I make my artwork, I create every element in it, completely,” he explained. “This time, the elements were created by other people; I just needed it all to make sense together and shape this narration between them.” When Shawky was approached to manage the fair, he thought it was a “healthy” opportunity to step away from his role as an artist and concentrate on others’ creativity. “I was even involved in the process of how to place artists beside each other,” he added. “I really enjoyed every moment, and for me, it was like making a big art piece.”

**If you happen to be visiting the fair, below are four artists to keep an eye on.**

### Aiza Ahmed


[#image: /photos/69831ad012d70f6cb68130e0]

+++inset-right

[#image: /photos/69831f362023714cf83c8a80]

+++

Originally from Pakistan, [the emerging artist](https://www.aizaahmed.com/){: target="_blank"} Aiza Ahmed, with gallery Sargent’s Daughters, reflects on themes of borders, performance and masculinity through her installation, *Footnotes.* It brings out different aspects of her practice, ranging from painting to sculpture and drawing. The installation, which was created in a residency at Doha’s art space Fire Station, comprises suspending muslin cloths set up with theatrical staging. The cloth pieces depict male soldiers performing a ceremony at the Wagah–Attari border between India and Pakistan.

"It’s a spectacle of theatricality, pomp and drama," Ahmed told *AD*, adding that she also aimed to trace her ancestry through the piece. “I’m thinking about the soldiers’ expression, gaze and how they are communicating with each other.”

### Lina Gazzaz


[#image: /photos/69831acb2023714cf83c8a7c]

+++inset-left

[#image: /photos/69831f8812d70f6cb68130e2]

+++

Another eye-catching installation comes from the Saudi artist Lina Gazzaz, who is presenting *Tracing Lines of Growth* with Hafez Gallery, highlighting the [palm tree](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/five-designers-in-alula-turn-palm-fibre-and-industrial-waste-into-furniture){: target="_blank"}, a significant and life-giving symbol of the Arabian Peninsula. The imposing, almost-sculptural installation comprises palm tree leaves attached with vibrant red threads, demonstrating the idea of the palm tree’s growth and longevity, akin to the human body. It is also tied to the notions of memory, time, fragility, struggle and resilience.

### Rashid Rana


[#image: /photos/69831acbe678a6a4122d1a6b]

+++inset-right

[#image: /photos/698325d112d70f6cb68130e4]

+++

A [photo-based artist](https://rashidrana.com/){: target="_blank"} from Pakistan, [Rashid Rana,](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/capitalism-and-the-global-south-artist-rashid-ranas-dubai-exhibition-features-90000-images-of-trash){: target="_blank"} highlights the genocide in Gaza through a detailed photo montage presentation called *Fractured Moment*, which he created in 2025. Covering the entirety of the Chemould Prescott Road gallery booth’s wall, the work comprises small light and dark squares that document the passage of time during a period of two hours during the night in Gaza. The work starts out in dark tones and ascends into bright orange and red colours, indicating moments of bombardment by Israeli forces. "It’s basically telling you about the fact that the war is live and still continues, and you’re seeing it as it happens. It is a real-time moment in history,” the gallery’s director, Shireen Gandhy, explained to *AD.* “I think it’s about time that artists really speak about it, and a certain amount of humanity is needed at this time. Who, if not artists, can speak about it?”

### Solange Pessoa


[#image: /photos/69831acb12d70f6cb68130de]

+++inset-left

[#image: /photos/698324202c80cac1e167577f]

+++

A former participant at the world-renowned Venice Biennale, [the Brazilian artist](https://mendeswooddm.com/artists/12-solange-pessoa/){: target="_blank"} Solange Pessoa is showcasing a trio of large-scale, orange-brown-toned paintings that were created with soil, affirming her longtime practice of working with organic materials. For these earthy works, depicting snake-like shapes, fruits and animals, Pessoa was inspired by the theme of cycles of life, as well as the natural scenery of her hometown of Minas Gerais in south-eastern Brazil. On the ground at the booth of Mendes Wood DM, there is also a display of her sculptural, ecological work. [*@artbasel*](https://www.instagram.com/artbasel/?hl=en){: target="_blank"}

title: These Puglia-Style Huts Are Your Private Pass to the African Wilderness
content:
**Who doesn't love the African wilderness? Now, you can enjoy it from a truly luxurious villa that genuinely respects the planet.**

Just a few kilometres upstream, where the gurgling rapids of the Zambezi River take a breather and hippos squabble, a cluster of cone-shaped straw roofs with lime-washed walls and pastel-coloured windows appears. It is difficult to place whether the setting belongs to the pantomime world of a Mediterranean fairy tale or recalls the Trulli – traditional dry-stone, triangular-roofed dwellings found in the Valle d’Itria region of Puglia, Italy.

But Mpala Jena Private Villas are located in Zimbabwe, [Africa](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/22-african-artists-confront-colonial-violence-in-new-exhibition){: target="_blank"}, sans the cobbled pathways and manicured order of Italy. And yet the well-worn Italian cliché, *la dolce far niente*, the sweetness of doing nothing, feels uncannily relevant here.​



[#image: /photos/698474199c0690dd5a7b297c]

What the setting lacks in formal landscaping, it compensates for [in wilderness](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/discover-the-most-zen-hotels-immersed-in-nature-for-wellness-loving-travellers){: target="_blank"}. Unruly bush vegetation envelops the villas, while river light filters through the leaves. Elephants and zebras occasionally wander down to the waterhole outside the villas, framed by large windows that open onto the landscape. One can sit on the couch, wrapped in a cosy blanket and reading a book, watching as the animals pause for an unhurried aperitif at the water’s edge.

These [Puglia-style](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/this-puglia-hotel-convent-turned-tobacco-factory-layers-monastic-calm-with-italian-ease){: target="_blank"} private huts are located approximately five kilometres from the main Mpala Jena camp, set along the banks of the Zambezi River within Zambezi National Park, near Victoria Falls in Zimbabwe.



[#image: /photos/6984741dee8113e8f04e41ce]

​Historically, the Trulli were used in 14th-century Italy as temporary structures by peasants as refuges from the sun, and by landowners for storage. Over time, they became a permanent fixture and ultimately the defining architectural identity of the region. Mpala Jena Private Villas, however, are no modest peasant shelters. These villas form a plush, modern address for discerning [wildlife safari](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/best-african-safaris-for-wildlife-lovers-and-thrill-seekers){: target="_blank"} enthusiasts under the Great Plains Conservation umbrella, opened in August 2025.



[#image: /photos/6984741eee8113e8f04e41cf]

The property is a pair of villas branching off from a central thatched pavilion via raised boardwalks. Each villa is a self-sustaining cocoon of luxury that comes with a private swimming pool, a freestanding bathtub, and three luxurious riverfront suites, as well as a private butler and guide for game drives.​

[Mpala Jena Private Villas](https://greatplainsconservation.com/mpala-jena-private-villas/){: target="_blank"} fall under the umbrella of Great Plains Conservation, the African eco-tourism organisation founded by National Geographic filmmaker explorers Dereck and Beverly Joubert. Sustainability in materials and a design philosophy that blurs the boundary between indoors and outdoors have long been hallmarks across their 15 properties in Botswana, Zimbabwe, and Kenya.



[#image: /photos/69847418053787875e772f4a]

​The camp was designed by owners Kevin and Shannon Lang in collaboration with architect Julia Rutherford and award-winning interior designer Tracy Kelly. Kelly, the [principal designer](https://www.tracykellydesign.com/){: target="_blank"} of the Tracy Kelly Design firm, wanted the villas to be rooted in authenticity and restraint, drawing on the vernacular architecture of Puglia and Alberobello, which is time-tested for its simplicity, solidity and sculptural clarity. The intention was to incorporate [passive design strategies](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/is-passive-house-design-the-future-of-sustainable-architecture){: target="_blank"}, maximise natural ventilation and admit generous daylight, reducing reliance on artificial systems.



[#image: /photos/69847418fff23c082fb9f26e]

A primordial thread of the project is the [biophilic design](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/7-diy-biophilic-design-ideas-you-can-recreate-at-home){: target="_blank"}. It is experienced in open patios, ungrilled windows, long sightlines, and circular roof apertures above the showers. From these openings, a lilac-breasted roller or a malachite kingfisher peers in from above. At Mpala Jena Private Villas, it is a sense of permeability that takes you by surprise. The expansive nature of the African wilderness seeps in through light, air, sound and feathered visitors, until it becomes an intimate, daily companion, even while you remain indoors.



[#image: /photos/69847427fff23c082fb9f276]

The furnishings do the same justice. Natural fibres, textured rugs and [lime-washed surfaces](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/this-earthy-apartment-with-limewash-interiors-gives-summer-holiday-vibes-all-year-round){: target="_blank"} are tempered with African craft references to anchor the spaces within their setting. Rope-wrapped furniture, handwoven raffia and palm fibres used as fringes for bespoke chandeliers, inverted basket weaves as lighting fixtures, reed-lined ceilings, and macramé ottomans contribute to the tactile finishes of the interiors, all produced locally in Swaziland.



[#image: /photos/69847423053787875e772f51]

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/69847424053787875e772f53]

[#image: /photos/698474239c0690dd5a7b2983]

+++

​As Kelly explains, each villa responds individually to its immediate environment. Villa 1 adopts a palette of layered greens drawn from the surrounding African mahogany trees. Villa 2 chimes towards the mood board of a woody spectrum inspired by the bushland in which it sits, ranging from biscuit-beige to earthy clay or [Pantone colour of 2026, Cloud Dancer](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/pantone-colour-of-the-year-2026-meet-cloud-dancer-the-softest-shade-of-white-yet){: target="_blank"}.

A bespoke leaf motif recurs throughout the project, appearing across multiple interior applications, developed directly from the African wilderness' tree canopies to establish a visual link between architecture and environment.



[#image: /photos/6984741ffff23c082fb9f270]

In Villa 2, Suzani bedcovers introduce traditional Suzani patterns in the language of embroidered leaf forms derived from foliage collected on-site. The same leaves appear as framed wall art.

Kelly says, “It was difficult to achieve a balance of creating interiors that feel considered rather than styled, luxurious without excess, and culturally grounded yet contemporary. But when I see the final result, I love how the mix of old and new, cultural and weathered elements came together within the natural landscape.” *[@tracykellydesign\_](https://www.instagram.com/tracykellydesign_/){: target="_blank"}, [@mpalajena\_zambezi](https://www.instagram.com/mpalajena_zambezi/){: target="_blank"}*

title: Janu Dubai Promises To Slow Down Sky-High City Living
content:
Longstanding as the city’s primary financial enclave, Dubai International Financial Centre has been stepping into a new chapter as a cultural and lifestyle destination. Within that context comes Janu Dubai, a new hotel and residential development bringing together international architecture firm Herzog & de Meuron and the Janu brand, the more energetic hospitality and lifestyle arm of the Aman Group.

With both making their Dubai debuts, it is a pairing that clearly carries weight. Yet what makes the project feel particularly notable is [the architectural stance](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/the-tallest-buildings-in-dubai-you-must-visit){: target="_blank"} it intends to take within the city. As the architects describe it, the scheme, conceived as a pair of glistening yet intentionally “porous” towers, is not an attempt to outshine the bravado of Dubai’s skyline, but rather to slow down and reconsider how architecture truly participates within it. “Unlike more typical reflective glass towers on parking plinths, this architecture emphasises mineral presence and lived experience – grounded and responsive to context,” the team at [Herzog & de Meuron](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/herzog-and-de-meuron-turn-one-roof-for-lombard-odier-a-swiss-bank-hq-into-a-flexible-human-centred-workplace){: target="_blank"} tells *AD*.



[#image: /photos/698b4d9bdfc6ee9f9b219bfe]



[#externallink: /externallinks/650303f6bb917946d989f4c0]

This sensitivity runs throughout the project, with the architecture paying close attention to the desert climate, the city’s more recent built heritage, and the particular ways in which local communities live. “Drawing inspiration from the climate-appropriate vernacular architecture of the region, the soft quality of [desert light](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/step-inside-a-desert-home-carved-on-top-of-an-ancient-black-lava-field-in-utahs-mojave-desert){: target="_blank"} and the textured facades of Dubai’s early modernist structures, the design is shaped by performance,” the team shares with *AD*. These considerations come through elements such as self-shading massing and wind-channelling forms, which reduce solar gain and make use of prevailing breezes while enhancing comfort. As the architects note, the design is intended to be “grounded rather than glossy, porous rather than sealed,” placing lived experience and climate adaptability at the forefront despite the project’s scale and monumentality.



[#image: /photos/698b4d67dfc6ee9f9b219bf8]

Beyond the image, there is also a notable interest for both teams in how the building will be occupied, particularly in terms of comfort and sociability, which can be a challenge to calibrate within the model of vertical architecture. With the city’s skyline forging ahead in increasingly bold expressions, the project holds on to cultural resonance without letting go of ambition or any of the exceptional standards of luxury living promised by the Aman and Janu names. “Embracing a meaningful connection to nature, it will integrate expansive outdoor terraces and balconies enriched with abundant planting and gardens,” shares Vlad Doronin, Aman’s CEO and Chairman, “creating a verdant, pedestrian-focused environment that is rare for a high-rise in the UAE.”



[#image: /photos/698b4d992f8e71314179c9a9]

One of the most striking features is an 18-metre high lobby that rises beside a core souk courtyard, above which the 150 hotel units stretch across ten levels, while 57 branded residences sit right at the top of the building’s most panoramic levels. From its scenic upper terraces, the views stretch across the neighbouring Zabeel Palace Gardens and Downtown Dubai – with the [Burj Khalifa](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/burj-khalifa-the-worlds-highest-penthouse-and-other-secrets-of-the-worlds-tallest-building-revealed){: target="_blank"} in clear, uninterrupted view – with selected units able to enjoy these views from the comfort of their own private pools and gardens.



[#image: /photos/698b4d9b0ce86365a3a755e3]

Inside, the residential component ranges from two- to five-bedroom apartments to a single triplex penthouse occupying the tower’s uppermost floors, and the interiors are completed in alignment with Aman’s philosophy to foster warmth and serenity across all of its retreats, and the Janu branch’s ambition to energise and inspire.

Residents of Janu Dubai can enjoy direct access to the hotel’s amenities, including expansive wellness facilities spanning over 1,700 square metres, multiple dining hotspots, and the newly-introduced global Janu Club, which is an invitation-only members’ space. *[@herzogdemeuron](https://www.instagram.com/herzogdemeuron/){: target="_blank"}, [@januhotels](https://www.instagram.com/januhotels/){: target="_blank"}*

## Also read: [This Chic Duplex in Dubai Is Proof That Customised Details Are the Next Big Trend in Luxury Living](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/chic-duplex-in-dubai-customised-details-lana-residences-big-trend-in-luxury-living){: target="_blank"}

title: La mejor forma de dormir: Esta es la temperatura ideal para el dormitorio
content:
**La mejor forma de dormir en invierno probablemente no sea la que piensas. Estos son los factores más importantes para garantizar un sueño óptimo.**

No hay nada más molesto que despertarse sudoroso y pegajoso bajo una manta caliente. De igual forma, es muy desagradable tiritar y congelarse en una habitación que está demasiado fría. En ambos casos, el sueño reparador queda descartado. **La temperatura del dormitorio influye mucho en la experiencia del sueño** y, por ende, en el bienestar físico. Ahora bien, ¿cuál es esa temperatura perfecta para el dormitorio?

## La temperatura óptima para dormir
Según la recomendación de la Agencia Alemana de Medio Ambiente (UBA), se sitúa entre 17 y 20 grados centígrados. Sin embargo, también depende de las preferencias personales: ¿eres fan del aire fresco o más bien friolento? Los expertos en sueño afirman que tanto las temperaturas más bajas como las más altas pueden ser adecuadas, siempre que el sueño sea reparador y te despiertes con energía. A manera orientativa, se recomienda una temperatura de entre 20 y 23 grados en el baño y la sala, de 18 a 20 grados en la cocina y de 15 a 18 grados en el pasillo.



[#image: /photos/6978ed01c3caa5efaee11b19]|||Incluso si estás resfriado, es importante que la habitación no esté demasiado caliente y se mantenga dentro del rango recomendado.|||

## ¿Por qué es tan importante la temperatura adecuada en el dormitorio?
Una temperatura adecuada ayuda a que tu temperatura corporal baje naturalmente antes de dormir, [hace que tu cuerpo se relaje](https://www.admagazine.com/articulos/colores-relajantes-para-el-dormitorio) y le da a tu organismo la señal de que comience su "programa nocturno". Por lo tanto, **es fundamental que en el dormitorio no haga demasiado calor ni demasiado frío.**

Evitando las temperaturas inferiores a 15 grados en el dormitorio se previene, entre otras cosas, la formación de moho en el espacio. Para ello se recomienda una temperatura ambiente constante de 18 o 19 grados, mientras que, para evitar la formación de moho general, la temperatura de la vivienda no debe bajar de 14 grados.

Por el contrario, las temperaturas ambiente excesivamente altas aumentan el riesgo de resfriados y supone un gasto innecesario de energía y dinero. **Por cada grado que bajes la temperatura de tu habitación, ahorrarás alrededor de un 6% en los gastos de calefacción**. Si bajas la temperatura de tu dormitorio de los habituales 25 grados a los 17-20 recomendados, no solo estarás haciendo algo bueno para tu salud sino también para tu bolsillo y el medio ambiente.

## La mejor temperatura para dormir según la estación
Para la mayoría de la gente, las altas temperaturas impiden un sueño reparador. En los calurosos meses de verano, una temperatura ambiente de entre 17 y 20 grados sigue siendo óptima y agradable para la mayoría. Lo mismo ocurre en el caso del invierno: entre 17 y 20 grados es lo ideal en las zonas de estar y de dormir, y nunca debe descender de los 14 grados.



[#image: /photos/6419d22266db29cbdee8df77]|||Las temperaturas ambiente excesivamente altas aumentan el riesgo de resfriados y supone un gasto innecesario de energía y dinero.|||

## ¿Cuál es la temperatura ideal si estás resfriado?
Incluso si estás resfriado, es importante que la habitación no esté demasiado caliente y se mantenga dentro del rango recomendado. De lo contrario, fácilmente puedes experimentar sudores nocturnos. El aire seco de la calefacción, en particular, puede irritar las vías respiratorias y favorecer el dolor de garganta. También debes asegurarte de ventilar el espacio con regularidad, pero cuidado: tampoco debes enfriar tu cuerpo en exceso. Tápate bien y busca una posición ligeramente elevada para dormir.

## ¿Y en el caso de los bebés y niños?
La temperatura ideal para la habitación de bebés y niños no difiere mucho de la temperatura ideal para los adultos. Por lo general, **los bebés y los niños prefieren dormir a temperaturas más frescas** —entre 16 y 19 grados Celsius—, por lo que sus dormitorios no deben ser demasiado cálidos.

En el caso particular de los bebés, los expertos indican que se recalientan cinco veces más rápido que los adultos. Para determinar la temperatura correcta del dormitorio, puedes medirla con un termómetro o con un monitor de bebés que tenga indicador de temperatura integrado.

Adicionalmente, ni las camas de los bebés ni de los niños deben colocarse directamente junto a la calefacción. La humedad en el dormitorio es tan esencial para los pequeños como para los adultos.



[#image: /photos/6914de173684fc94ece1f9ba]|||Evita la humedad excesiva en el dormitorio con una ventilación correcta.|||

## La humedad óptima en el dormitorio
La temperatura del dormitorio no es el único factor que influye en un sueño saludable, [también lo es el clima de la habitación](https://www.admagazine.com/articulos/telas-que-evitan-el-calor). El nivel óptimo de humedad en el dormitorio oscila entre el 40% y el 50% (ten en cuenta que una humedad excesiva puede favorecer la aparición de moho). Si los cristales de las ventanas se empañan desde dentro y forman condensación, es señal de que la humedad es demasiado alta.

El moho no solo daña materiales como las juntas de silicona y el papel tapiz, sino que también es especialmente perjudicial para la salud. **Evita la humedad excesiva en el dormitorio con una ventilación correcta.**

### La ventilación adecuada según la UBA
- Ventila las habitaciones al menos una vez al día (preferiblemente varias veces) durante cinco a diez minutos, por ejemplo el dormitorio justo después de levantarte.
- Asegúrate de que haya corriente de aire al ventilar abriendo ventanas opuestas.
- Ventila siempre que se produzca vapor de agua en el hogar, por ejemplo al cocinar, bañarte o secar la ropa.
- Ventila también con regularidad las habitaciones que se utilicen poco.
- Durante el invierno apaga la calefacción cuando las ventanas estén abiertas.



[#image: /photos/635bfb7d8b465ad04ec2d2b3]|||La temperatura ambiente óptima para garantizar la mejor forma de dormir en invierno es de al menos 17 grados.|||

## Cómo bajar la temperatura ambiente en verano
En verano, las altas temperaturas pueden provocar noches de insomnio. Puedes contrarrestar esto ventilando bien tu dormitorio a primera hora de la mañana y luego oscureciendo las ventanas con persianas o cortinas. De esta manera, se minimiza la entrada de calor del exterior durante el día. Tampoco debes subestimar las fuentes de calor de aparatos electrónicos como televisores o cargadores; es mejor mantenerlos alejados del dormitorio cuando la temperatura es alta.

## Cómo calentar correctamente en invierno
No dejes nunca que la temperatura de tu dormitorio descienda por debajo del punto de congelación durante los meses fríos del año. Según los expertos, **la temperatura ambiente óptima para garantizar la mejor forma de dormir en invierno es de al menos 17 grados.**

Asimismo, debes mantener suficientemente cálidas todas las habitaciones del hogar. Durante el día, mantén la calefacción a una temperatura constante y cierra las puertas de las habitaciones donde no haya calefacción. A la hora de dormir, baja la calefacción al mínimo y ventila bien la habitación antes de acostarte.

*Artículo publicado originalmente en* **AD *Alemania.***

title: Les 10 projets les plus spectaculaires de Frank Gehry, de Paris à Los Angeles
content:
[Frank Gehry](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/hommage-frank-gehry-architecte-96-ans), l'architecte américain légendaire né au Canada, dont l'œuvre a élargi le domaine esthétique du design et provoqué *« l'effet Bilbao »*, est décédé à Santa Monica, en Californie, ce vendredi 5 décembre. Il avait 96 ans.

Le travail de [Frank Gehry](https://www.admagazine.fr/adinspiration/article/frank-gehry-portrait-architecte-designer) a toujours été axé sur l'architecture en tant que sculpture ; devant le jury Pritzker en 1989, il a cité l'artiste Constantin Brâncuși comme une source d'inspiration plus importante que de nombreux architectes de l'époque. Cependant, il ne considérait pas que son travail était uniquement basé sur la forme et reconnaissait que l'architecture avait aussi des exigences fonctionnelles qui étaient intrinsèquement nécessaires. *« J'aborde chaque bâtiment comme un objet sculptural, un contenant spatial, un espace avec de la lumière et de l'air, une réponse au contexte et à l'adéquation du sentiment et de l’esprit »*, a-t-il déclaré un jour. *« Dans ce contenant, cette sculpture, l'utilisateur apporte son bagage, son programme, et interagit avec lui pour répondre à ses besoins. S'il ne peut pas le faire, j'ai échoué. »*

## Les 10 projets les plus spectaculaires de Frank Gehry entre Paris, Arles, Bilbao…

title: Trendfarben 2026: Diese Farbtöne sind im kommenden Jahr angesagt – laut AD100-Designer:innen
content:
**Trendfarben 2026: AD100-Designer:innen verraten ihre Favoriten für das kommende Jahr.**

Es gibt bekanntlich zwei Arten, wie mit der [Wandgestaltung](https://www.ad-magazin.de/artikel/waende-streichen-tipps){: target="_blank"} umgegangen wird. Entweder, man hält es einfach und schlicht, und entscheidet sich für einen weißen Wandanstrich (und das meist in der gesamten Wohnung) oder aber, man ist wagemutig und taucht jeden Raum in eine andere Nuance. Nun lässt sich weder das eine, noch das andere, als „richtig“ kategorisieren. Nun mag Weiß mit großer Wahrscheinlichkeit der [Farbton](https://www.ad-magazin.de/artikel/grundlagen-farbenlehre-fuer-inneneinrichtung){: target="_blank"} sein, der die meisten Wände deutscher Wohnräume bedeckt, doch lässt sich seit einigen Jahren ein mutigerer Umgang mit Wandfarben erkennen. Und das ist toll, schließlich kann die Wahl der Wandfarbe die Stimmung im Raum erheblich beeinflussen. Der Schweizer Architekt Le Corbusier ging sogar so weit und behauptete, dass Farben in der Architektur ein ebenso wichtiges Mittel seien wie der Grundriss oder der Schnitt. Dabei hört die Farbgestaltung noch lange nicht beim Kauf des Farbeimers auf. Genauso wichtig wie der Ton sind Muster, Maserungen und Oberflächen, die das Farbkonzept am Ende dynamisch machen. Doch auf welche Farben, Muster und Kombinationen sollten wir 2026 setzen? Das weiß wohl niemand besser als unsere [AD100-Interiordesigner:innen](https://www.ad-magazin.de/ad100){: target="_blank"} – lassen Sie sich inspirieren\!

+++inset-left

[#externallink: /externallinks/674da354c166cd4b99f8e392]

+++

## Diese Trendfarben und Muster bestimmen laut AD100-Designer:innen das Jahr 2026
Wir haben vier unserer frisch gekürten AD100-Designer:innen gefragt, welche Farbtöne auf dem Trendradar 2026 ganz oben liegen. Ihre Lieblingsnuancen verraten wir hier.

### Warme Juweltöne
In den vergangenen Jahren hat sich eine äußerst elegante Form des [Maximalismus](https://www.ad-magazin.de/artikel/stil-trend-maximalismus){: target="_blank"} in unseren Interiors breit gemacht und Räume hervorgebracht, die geprägt sind von Farben, Extravaganz, Kühnheit und Humor. Gleichzeitig ließ sich eine Zuwendung zu warmen Erdtönen beobachten: Warme Brauntöne zeichnen die Saison ebenso wie die breite Palette an Grün und Rot. Für die AD100-Designerin Stephanie Thatenhorst tritt dieser Hang zur Sinnlichkeit 2026 noch stärker hervor. „Für mich bewegt sich 2026 ganz klar in Richtung tiefer, warmer Farbtöne: Burgund, intensives Rot bis hin zu leicht orangen Nuancen, kombiniert mit dunklen Lila- und Pflaumentönen – Farben mit einer spürbaren Wärme und Tiefe. Sie bringen eine Erdung in Räume, wirken aber gleichzeitig kraftvoll und emotional.“ Dazu empfiehlt Thatenhorst die Kombination mit natürlichen Materialien. „Das verleiht diesen Tönen eine besondere Präsenz.“



[#image: /photos/6931979b655cd3ef2f9bf6b5]|||Laut der AD100-Designerin Stephanie Thatenhorst sind warme Erdtöne 2026 auf dem Vormarsch. Im Zusammenspiel mit natürlichen Materialien entfalten sie ihr volles Potenzial.|||

### Goldenes Camouflage
Die Gestaltung mit Farbe ist das eine, doch lässt der Umgang durchaus Spielraum zu. Für den AD100-Designer [Fabian Freytag](https://www.ad-magazin.de/artikel/altbau-berlin-charlottenburg-fabian-freytag){: target="_blank"} steht eines fest: „Ich bin überzeugt, dass wir künftig vermehrt Muster sehen werden, die sich an Camouflage anlehnen – allerdings nicht im Sinne klassischer olivgrüner Tarnfarben, sondern als monochrome Mélange aus drei aufeinander abgestimmten Tönen.“ Es muss also nicht immer nur klassisch einfarbig sein. „Gerade in Kombination mit glänzenden Oberflächen entsteht eine Atmosphäre, die Geborgenheit ausstrahlt. Vielleicht würde ich diesen Trend ‚Golden Camouflage‘ nennen: eine harmonisch orchestrierte Farbwelt – ein wenig spießig, aber durchaus stolz.“



[#image: /photos/6931974c655cd3ef2f9bf6b3]|||Laut Fabian Freytag feiert das Camouflage-Muster 2026 ein Comeback – und zwar ein schillerndes, glänzendes.|||

### Mineralische Blaugrün-Nuancen
Die AD100-Interiordesignerin [Sophie Ashby](https://www.ad-magazin.de/artikel/sophie-ashby-wohnhaus-london){: target="_blank"} prognostiziert für 2026 eine Palette aus mineralischen Blau- und Grünnuancen: „In 2026 zieht es mich zu einer blau-grünen Farbfamilie aus satten Kobalt- und Kornblumenblau, die gleichermaßen präsent und beruhigend wirken. Sie besitzen eine juwelenartige Tiefe, lassen Räume lebendig erscheinen, und vermitteln das Gefühl von Klarheit und Heiterkeit – wie ein Abbild von Himmel und Wasser.“ Besonders raffiniert ist für die Interiordesignerin die Kombination mit tiefen Schokoladentönen. „[Brauntöne](https://www.ad-magazin.de/artikel/trendfarben-beige-braun-wohnung-einrichten){: target="_blank"} schaffen in diesem Zusammenspiel mit diesen lebendigen, energiegeladenen Akzenten Wärme und Geborgenheit und runden das Farbkonzept harmonisch ab.“ Dazu kommen dunkle, fruchtige Rottöne, die für eine gewisse Prise Drama sorgen. „Das erzeugt eine stimmungsvolle Fülle – ideal für vielschichtige, atmosphärische Innenräume“, weiß Ashby. Den finalen Abschluss des eleganten Farbkonzeptes bildet ein zartes Lila, „das eine sanfte, helle und luftige Note einbringt und in Kontrast zu den satten, erdigen Nuancen nahezu verträumt wirkt.“



[#image: /photos/693198af655cd3ef2f9bf6b7]|||Die AD100-Interiordesignerin Sophie Ashby setzt auf mineralische Blau- und Grünnuancen, die im Zusammenspiel mit roten Akzenten eine wunderbar lebhafte Atmosphäre schaffen – so wie hier in diesem Londoner Schlafzimmer.|||

### Dynamische Farbwelten
„Wir haben das Gefühl, dass weniger eine Farbe an sich die Trends 2026 bestimmen wird, sondern vielmehr das Mixen von Farbstimmungen“, beobachtet das [AD100-Designerinnenduo Antonius Schimmelbusch](https://www.ad-magazin.de/artikel/lokale-talente-ad100){: target="_blank"}. „Zumindest wünschen wir uns das.“ Zu einem übergreifenden Farbton gesellen sich also immer wieder kleine, unterschiedliche Akzente. „Bei einem aktuellen Projekt, ein Produzenten-Loft in Prenzlauer Berg, sieht man gut, was wir meinen: Wir haben einen sehr dunklen Farbkanon ausgewählt und immer wieder fast schon grelle Akzente eingebaut, die den *vibey* Farbkanon unterbrechen und damit noch verstärken. In der Küche das Arsenic Green, vor der Plattenwand leuchten blaue Samtsessel oder im Text-Zimmer eine fast neongelbe Mohairdecke.“ Weniger ist nicht automatisch mehr – so das Fazit.



[#image: /photos/69319707655cd3ef2f9bf6af]|||In diesem Produzenten-Loft in Prenzlauer Berg, gestaltet von Antonius Schimmelbusch, treffen leuchtend blaue Samtsessel auf eine terracottarote Plattenwand.|||

## Die AD100-Talente im Video kennenlernen

title: Meet the architects and designers who design the homes of Indian celebrities
content:
Beyond the red carpets and flashing lights, Indian celebrities retreat to sanctuaries designed by the country's (and beyond) most talented interior designers and architects. In this exclusive peek behind the velvet ropes, we'll meet the creative minds who transform dreams into a spectacular reality. Read on to uncover the design secrets that make India's popular stars shine even brighter at home.

## Gauri Khan, Gauri Khan Designs


[#image: /photos/6643a5d2265ca80b06be8e46]|||*Gauri Khan with Ananya Panday.*|||

Gauri Khan Designs was founded in 2013 in Mumbai. A leading name in the interior design industry, Gauri Khan has turned heads for her focus of crafting spaces boasting a luxurious and elegant design aesthetic. Khan has spearheaded the interior design of numerous celebrity homes, including [Ananya Panday’s new abode](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/inside-ananya-pandays-first-home-in-mumbai-designed-by-gauri-khan/){: target="_blank"} and [Karan Johar’s home renovation](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/karan-johars-bachelor-pad-in-mumbai-is-as-glamorous-as-his-bollywood-films-celebrity-homes-gauri-khan-homes/){: target="_blank"}.

“Design has always piqued my interest,” expresses the designer, “I have a background in graphic design and have worked with charcoal and oil painting, which has helped me refine my skills. But it wasn't until I was designing my house, Mannat, that I realised design is my calling. Striking the perfect balance between adding aesthetics and factoring in the clients' brief, while transforming spaces is Gauri Khan Designs' major USP. Additionally, we possess the ability to elevate any space into an imaginative, thoughtful and compelling environment that feels bespoke, and is a reflection of its people.”

Also read: [*6 famous homes and stores designed by AD100 designer Gauri Khan*](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/famous-homes-and-stores-designed-by-ad100-designer-gauri-khan-shah-rukh-khan-home/){: target="_blank"}

## Sarah Sham, Essajees Atelier


[#image: /photos/6643a659265ca80b06be8e48]|||*Sarah Sham with Ishan Khatter.*|||

Mumbai-based Sarah Sham is the founder of Essajees Atelier, which is renowned for crafting timeless homes that showcase the clients’ personality. In Sarah’s 15 years of work experience, she has crafted the interiors of several high-profile spaces such as [Ishan Khatter’s home](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/ishaan-khatter-mumbai-apartment-is-a-sunset-sanctuary-celebrity-homes/){: target="_blank"}.

“My personal approach to work, which naturally influences all of Essajees Atelier’s endeavours, revolves around consistently aiming to provide top-notch décor solutions. This is achieved through close collaboration with our clients and by offering extensive customisation to fulfil their specific requirements. In terms of Essajees Atelier’s signature style, I think the one thing that we prioritise over all else is to maintain a sense of elegance and timelessness in every home we design. We take great pains to create spaces that go beyond passing design trends, ensuring longevity beyond the transient styles that may fade within a few years,” mentions Sarah Sham, interior designer and founder of Essajees Atelier.

## reD Architects


[#image: /photos/6643a6ae1825a14bc3f6df59]|||*Rajiv and Ekta Parekh with Sonakshi Sinha.*|||

reD Architects is a design and architecture studio that has been instrumental in creating the interiors of a number of celebrity homes such as that of [Sonakshi Sinha](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/sonakshi-sinhas-4000-square-foot-mumbai-home-is-an-embodiment-of-understated-luxury-red-architects-rajiv-ekta-parekh/){: target="_blank"}. At the heart of the ethos at reD Architects lies a philosophy that amalgamates contextual, vernacular, and contemporary elements. The Mumbai-based studio’s design language resonates with the surroundings, embracing local flavours while infusing modern sensibilities. Each project, be it residential or commercial unfolds as a narrative crafted from the fabric of its context.

“We view design as a collaborative symphony. Our team seamlessly collaborates with artists, sculptors, engineers, and various design experts. This convergence of diverse perspectives infuses each project with nuanced insights, enriching the design process and ensuring comprehensive solutions that cater to the diverse perceptions of our clients and users. Purpose-driven design isn’t just a concept for us, it’s a philosophy ingrained in every blueprint, every structure, and every interaction. We strive to achieve our clients’ goals through the process of research,” mention Rajiv Parekh, Ekta Parekh and Mithali Raut, founders of reD Architects.

## Quirk Studio


[#image: /photos/6643a72ea4d606164a1e0ac3]|||*Disha Bhavsar and Shivani Ajmera with Aditya Seal and and Anushka Ranjan.*|||

Quirk Studio is a boutique interior design firm based out of Mumbai. Founded in 2013 by Disha Bhavsar and Shivani Ajmera, the studio finds its roots in the confluence of the design ideologies of the duo. Striving towards an ethos that shapes people-centric spaces and environments, the design firm has conceptualised the interiors of [Aditya Seal and Anushka Ranjan Seal’s Mumbai home](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/inside-aditya-seal-and-anushka-ranjan-mumbai-home/){: target="_blank"}. Inspired and influenced by nature, Quirk Studio strives to establish a relationship with the surroundings, weaving a narrative befitting the physical context.

“Our design aesthetic sustains functionality while being classic in its appearance. Every project observes a distinct process of brainstorming that drives the design ideation, which is different for each project we undertake. Our approach is client-centric, process driven to learn and unlearn with every commission, resulting in an ever-growing and evolving practice,” remark Disha Bhavsar and Shivani Ajmera, founders of Quirk Studio.

## Rupin Suchak


[#image: /photos/68008ee28854073fec1fac3b]

Interior designer Rupin Suchak designed actor [Aparshakti Khurana's Mumbai abode](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/actor-aparshakti-khuranas-mumbai-home-displays-drama-in-the-details-designed-by-rupin-suchak/){: target="_blank"}. Suchak is well-known for crafting unorthodox spaces that are dramatic, exhibit the resident’s personality, and push the boundaries of conventional interior design.

“I like to get really involved in everything I do, and give my hundred percent to any project that I am doing. I also have a penchant for exploring madness in design. I do not see things they stereotypically should be seen. For me, design is about having fun and being confident, I don’t want to get bored with something I create. I never want to feel a lack of ownership for what I have made. Hence, right from receiving the brief, for whatever kind of spaces I might be working with, I like to start thinking a little off-beat. I follow this path, hoping the result excites me. For me, each subsequent project has to be better than the other one, especially in terms of the design value and process. I am growing in my design sensibilities with time and I believe that should reflect in every project I do, and of course, it should keep becoming more ‘mad’, in every way possible\!” states interior designer, Rupin Suchak.

## Phillippe Fouché, SAOTA


[#image: /photos/6643a8134b9db58e3f91bc0e]|||*Virat Kohli at his Alibaug villa.*|||

[Virat Kohli's Alibaug home](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/cricketer-virat-kohlis-holiday-home-in-alibag-is-the-embodiment-of-rustic-biophilic-luxury-celebrity-homes-vacation-homes-alibaug/){: target="_blank"} by Avās Living, created by globally acclaimed architects at SAOTA led by Phillippe Fouché, epitomises a biophilic design ethos, seamlessly blending modernity with nature-inspired elements. Nestled amidst the serene landscape of Alibaug, the overall design and style of the home exudes a modern yet contemporary vibe, characterised by natural light flowing in every corner, clean lines, sleek furnishings, and a calming colour palette.

## Shabnam Gupta, The Orange Lane
Over the last few decades, interior designer Shabnam Gupta has had several Indian celebrities for clients, including Aditya Chopra, Rajkumar Rao, Kangana Ranaut, Alia Bhatt and Parineeti Chopra. With a penchant for all things Indian, think bright colours, textures, and intricate aesthetics, Gupta started The Orange Lane, her design solution firm in 2003 that specialises in crafting exclusive home designs and furniture.

Shabnam’s design aesthetic is maximalist and eclectic, with a focus on incorporating traditional Indian artworks and natural elements. On how to design homes, Gupta advises homeowners to “be free to experiment\! Try to mix different cultures instead of sticking to a certain theme. If you do this, you will always end up giving a more diverse flavour to your home.”

## Kavita Singh, Kavita Singh Interiors


[#image: /photos/673c54dcc3ff95012fd5ab01]|||*Sonam Kapoor in the living room of her Mumbai home with her aunt and designer Kavita Singh.*|||

A true doyenne of design, AD100 designer Kavita Singh began her career three decades ago, when she founded her namesake brand of home decor accents in 1977. What began as a humble brand soon grew into a thriving practice—Singh’s tasteful eye was hard to miss, which was informed, over many years, by her deep interest in contemporary and decorative arts.

She collaborated with her niece, Sonam Kapoor, to bring the maximalist vision of [Kapoor's Mumbai home](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/sonam-kapoor-mumbai-home-is-a-maximalist-cocoon-of-indian-craftsmanship-and-heritage-celebrity-homes-ad100-interior-designer-kavita-singh/){: target="_blank"} to life. “Besides my nani, my aunt Kavita Singh has been the main voice within these walls, alongside my own,” says Kapoor. The pair threw themselves into transforming the contemporary blank canvas into, in Singh’s words, “the homage to Indian craftsmanship that my niece has been imagining for a long time”.

## Apoorva Shroff, Lyth Design


[#image: /photos/673c5ae8cd0c162a58f51b7b]|||*Madhuri Dixit with her husband, Dr Shriram Madhav Nene, and their Indie friend, Carmelo, in their Mumbai apartment.*|||

Madhuri Dixit and Dr Shriram Madhav Nene’s sea-facing apartment in Mumbai was designed by Apoorva Shroff of Lyth Design to be a union between art and technology. A clear brief helped Shroff create a home that oozes with understated elegance and simplicity. The couple requested "a sanctuary that blends contemporary aesthetics with a minimalist undertone, emphasising straight lines, clean forms, and a sober palette’’. This also convinced Shroff to use a “neutral interior palette that complements the homeowners' vibrant personalities.’’

## Annkur Khosla, Annkur Khosla Design Studio


[#image: /photos/68008f2b8854073fec1fac3d]|||*Mira in a printed co-ord set from Moonray in the formal living room. She is wearing heels from Aquazzura, earrings from Miss Jo, as well as rings from Misho and Miss Jo.*|||

The moment Mira Kapoor walked into the bare shell that is now her new [Mumbai abode](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/mira-and-shahid-kapoor-mumbai-home-is-all-about-wholesome-family-fun-with-design-at-its-heart/){: target="_blank"}, she knew she had come home. It took 14 long months, but with the help of AD100 designer Annkur Khosla—a designer who Shahid Kapoor had worked with previously— she was able to bring her vision to life. “She’s a Virgo; I loved that. And she manages to blend Shahid’s and my aesthetic—that is often quite opposing—together.” Mira describes her husband’s taste as avant-garde while she looks for practicality. “Will it age well? How do we get the most storage space? How do we clean this?” she laughs. “Annkur was a fair referee and listener. We decided that in the kitchen and the kids’ room, I had a veto; upstairs, Shahid had a veto, and whatever was left was under negotiation\!”

*Original text by Priyanka Khanna, edited for context.*

## Kush Bhayani, KULx Studio


[#image: /photos/68008f6950c932f7d0e5f1b0]|||*Salaam Namaste (2005) and Dhamaal (2007) actor and television host, Jaaved Jaaferi in his living room in Bandra.*|||

For the design of his [Bandra home](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/actor-jaaved-jaaferis-sea-facing-mumbai-home-is-a-mediterranean-oasis-made-of-memories-celebrity-homes-designed-by-kush-bhayani-of-kulx-studio/){: target="_blank"}, Jaaved Jaaferi approached Kush Bhayani of KULx Studio. After hours of discussion and brainstorming, his brief was to create a space with minimal sophistication. “Warm, natural, eclectic.’’ And so, Kush, with the help of Polyfloors India, designed a home that hangs heavy with history…a mature, expansive twin of his family home with Mediterranean design ethos.

Open communication is key for Kush Bhayani. Regular meetings and constructive inputs from Jaaved resulted in a well-balanced, harmonious home. While merging functionality with aesthetics raises a challenge, at the heart of the design stands individuality. “Incorporating personal touches and meaningful elements is the cornerstone of our design approach.’’ Mementos, favourite colours and patterns, and characteristic details of home dwellers aren’t just a requirement but also a design vernacular that resonates with Kush. He says, “Jaaved was part of the construction, actively present on site, helping us navigate a place he calls home.’’

*Original text by Harleen Kalsi, edited for context.*
