title: Un pequeño departamento usa la madera y el color en su diseño
content:
El edificio de este **pequeño departamento** data de 1968 y está situado en el barrio de Vavin, en la orilla izquierda de París. En la séptima planta, la estructura de hormigón del edificio permite una flexibilidad absoluta en la distribución del **pequeño departamento**: 69 metros cuadrados de tabiques sencillos, sin muros de carga. Al abrir todo el conjunto, la arquitecta Pauline Borgia ha podido beneficiarse de 12 metros lineales de fachada acristalada sobre París, con una vista despejada del cielo.

El reto de [este **pequeño departamento**](https://www.admagazine.com/articulos/pequeno-departamento-minimalista-un-hogar-espontaneo-y-fresco) consistía en multiplicar las funciones en un espacio reducido para crear una gran zona de estar compuesta por una sala, un comedor y una cocina americana; una zona de noche con dos dormitorios y un cuarto de baño; y espacios auxiliares como una lavandería, un tocador para invitados y una pequeña zona de oficinas. Una vez eliminados todos los tabiques, el arquitecto utilizó los materiales y el mobiliario integrado diseñado a medida para expresar los distintos espacios sin cerrarlos. Por ejemplo, el dormitorio, que es una continuación de la sala, está colocado sobre una plataforma con dos escalones enmoquetados que sirven de asiento para recibir a los invitados. Está cerrado por un juego de gruesas cortinas que, al descorrerse, dan la impresión de estar en una habitación aparte, protegida de la luz.



[#image: /photos/67d07af83e3dd6494b0a37f1]|||Alrededor de la mesa del comedor, de roble recuperado y mármol de Cipollino, se sientan sillas de Bruno Rey. Lámparas colgantes de una colección *vintage*. Detrás del banco está la sala. En la pared de la izquierda, una antigua colgadura Suzani (The House of Uzbekistan).|||

## Colores y materiales
Las paredes del dormitorio son de revestimiento de roble teñido de coñac, una madera que vuelve a la sala desde la sala de estar y recorre la cocina y la sala, como un hilo rojo. En los suelos de la cocina y el comedor, las teselas y el acero inoxidable de los muebles de cocina reflejan la luz, marcando los espacios y acentuando los puntos de fuga. Un salpicadero de plexiglás naranja separa la cocina del comedor. Refleja las vistas de las ventanas y confiere a la cocina un aire de cabina. Una vez más, su transparencia une la sala con la cocina. Mientras que la madera se utiliza para contrastar, dar calidez y delimitar [las funciones del **pequeño departamento**](https://www.admagazine.com/articulos/un-pequeno-departamento-que-es-un-oasis-de-elegancia-y-tranquilidad), la pintura azul claro se aplica de pared a techo para reflejar el cielo azul que se extiende a través de los grandes ventanales.

Todos los muebles, hechos a medida, también responden al deseo de secuenciar las distintas funciones del **pequeño departamento**: un banco en forma de L delimita la sala. Se contrapone a la gran isla de trabajo de la cocina y a otro banco en el comedor, en una disposición en T que aprovecha al máximo cada centímetro cuadrado, sin desperdiciar espacio. "Hemos diseñado este pequeño espacio como un barco o una gran embarcación en la que caben tantas funciones como sea posible", explica Pauline Borgia. Y con estilo.

*@pauline.borgia*



[#image: /photos/67d07af97c377c114895391e]|||La cocina está separada de la sala por la isla de acero inoxidable cepillado y su salpicadera de plexiglás naranja. Los frentes de la cocina son de chapa de roble teñido, al igual que la pared del fondo, con tiradores diseñados a medida. Apliques (Axel Chay). Pintura de Alékos Fassianós.|||



[#image: /photos/67d07af97c377c114895391f]|||La cabecera integrada, diseñada a medida, es de roble teñido. En sus hornacinas hay una escultura de porcelana de Afrodita hecha a mano (Marin Montagut) y *Una escultura de tierra* de Tim Leclabart (Galerie Mouvements Modernes). Jarrón *vintage* de Ikea. Aplique de cristal *vintage*. En la pared, un cuadro de Roger Tolmer.|||



[#image: /photos/67d07af9679ad539f8f3319f]|||
El mueble bajo lavabo, con su gran aparador de pared de madera de oukoumé recuperada, se diseñó a medida. Alberga un lavabo y una grifería *vintage* (de Leboncoin). Mosaico de loza amarilla (Bisazza).  
En la pared, fotografías de la serie *Tokyo silver* de Pauline Borgia.
|||

*Artículo publicado originalmente en* **AD *Francia***_._

title: Cómo crear un altar energético en casa para atraer paz y bienestar
content:
Si no sabes **cómo crear un altar energético en casa**, es más sencillo de lo que crees. En la búsqueda de armonía y equilibrio en nuestro hogar, un altar energético puede ser una herramienta poderosa. Un altar no solo embellece el espacio, sino que también actúa como un punto de concentración para nuestras intenciones espirituales y para tener un refugio especial en casa.

**Un altar es un reflejo de nuestra energía interior** y nos ayuda a conectar con lo divino. Puede ser utilizado para meditación, reflexión o simplemente para generar un ambiente de tranquilidad en el hogar. Su poder radica en los elementos que lo componen y en la intención con la que se crea.

Tener un altar también sirve como **recordatorio de nuestras** **metas y propósitos espirituales**. Cada vez que te acerques a él, podrás tomarte un momento para reconectar contigo misma y con tus deseos. Además, funciona como un refugio de paz en los momentos de estrés o incertidumbre.



[#image: /photos/67dc893200775a732e3572f6]

## ¿Cómo elegir el lugar ideal?
Para que un altar tenga la mayor efectividad, es importante seleccionar un lugar adecuado en casa. Puedes considerar…

- **Un rincón tranquilo y poco transitado:** un espacio donde no haya interrupciones constantes, permitiendo que la energía fluya con serenidad.
- **Un lugar con buena iluminación natural:** [la luz solar aporta energía positiva y vitalidad al altar](https://www.admagazine.com/articulos/decoracion-solar-como-incluir-la-energia-del-sol-en-tu-hogar).
- **Un espacio donde te sientas cómodo y en paz:** la conexión emocional con el lugar es esencial. Puedes decorarlo de una manera que refleje tu esencia.

**Evita colocar el altar en lugares muy cercanos a la puerta principal** o en espacios con mucho desorden. La energía del altar necesita estabilidad y calma.



[#image: /photos/67dc8a1d5db3175817934909]

## ¿Qué necesitas para crear tu altar energético?
Cada altar es único, pero hay ciertos elementos que potencian su energía.

- [**Velas:** representan la luz y la energía espiritual.](https://www.admagazine.com/articulos/que-color-de-vela-debes-tener-en-casa-segun-tus-objetivos) Pueden simbolizar la claridad mental y la iluminación de nuestro camino.
- **Cristales y piedras:** como amatista para la paz, cuarzo rosa para el amor o citrino para la prosperidad. Estos minerales tienen propiedades energéticas específicas.
- **Inciensos o sahumerios:** la purificación del espacio es esencial. Puedes usar palo santo, copal o lavanda según la intención que desees establecer.
- **Imágenes o figuras sagradas:** representaciones de deidades, ángeles o maestros espirituales pueden brindar protección y guía.
- **Plantas o flores:** aportan vida y frescura, además de purificar el aire. Las plantas como la lavanda o el bambú son especialmente recomendadas.
- **Tazón con agua o conchas marinas:** el agua simboliza la pureza y el flujo constante de energía. Puedes renovarla regularmente para mantener su frescura.



[#image: /photos/67dc8ac45db317581793490b]

## ¿Cómo activar tu altar energético en casa?
Una vez que el altar esté listo, es importante activarlo con una ceremonia sencilla.

- **Enciende una vela** y colócate frente al altar.
- **Respira profundamente** y visualiza una energía positiva rodeando el espacio.
- **Declara en voz alta tu intención para el altar.** Puede ser un deseo de [paz, amor, protección o cualquier otra aspiración](https://www.admagazine.com/editors-pick/como-y-por-que-hacer-un-tablero-de-inspiracion-20170712-3160-articulos).
- **Agradece** a las energías superiores por su protección y bendición.

Puedes acompañar este momento con música suave, meditación o cantos sagrados. La clave es que te sientas cómoda y conectada.



[#image: /photos/67dc8b5200775a732e3572f8]

## ¿Cómo mantener tu altar energético?
Para mantener la energía del altar, es clave limpiarlo regularmente.

- **Cambia el agua y las flores** cuando sea necesario.
- **Limpia los cristales** con agua y sal, o colócalos a la luz de la luna llena para recargarlos.
- **Renueva las velas y el incienso** según su uso. Si una vela se consume completamente, agradécele y reemplázala por una nueva.
- **Puedes sahumar el altar** con [hierbas como romero o salvia](https://www.admagazine.com/articulos/que-planta-tener-segun-tu-horoscopo-chino) para despejar cualquier energía estancada.

Este proceso no solo cuida de los elementos físicos, sino que también **renueva la intención del altar**.



[#image: /photos/67dc8bfe5db317581793490d]

## ¿Cómo hacer especial tu altar energético?
Dependiendo de lo que desees atraer, puedes modificar los elementos del altar.

- **Para paz interior:** usa amatista, lavanda y velas blancas. Puedes incluir una afirmación positiva o una imagen de la naturaleza.
- **Para amor propio:** agrega un cuarzo rosa, flores frescas y mantras inspiradores. Los espejos también son un símbolo de autoaceptación.
- **Para protección:** [el ónix o la obsidiana negra funcionan muy bien](https://www.admagazine.com/articulos/este-cuarzo-atrae-energia-y-buena-fortuna-todo-el-ano). Combínalos con sándalo y una imagen de un guía espiritual.

Recuerda que lo más importante es que tu altar refleje tu esencia y lo que deseas manifestar. Crear un altar energético en casa es **un acto de amor propio y de conexión espiritual**. Con los elementos adecuados y una intención clara, puedes transformar tu espacio en un refugio de paz. **Dedica unos minutos al día para conectarte con él** y notarás los cambios positivos en tu vida. Si permites que tu altar evolucione con tus necesidades, se convertirá en un compañero espiritual invaluable.

title: Tour a Modern Dubai Villa Where a Sculptural Staircase Sets the Scene
content:
There's a particular moment when visitors first step into this modern Dubai villa that captures the essence of what designers Meher Sahney and Bani Singh of GroundedDesign have achieved. As eyes travel upward, following the [sculptural sweep of a staircase that ribbons](https://www.admiddleeast.com/gallery/a-spiral-staircase-is-the-star-attraction-in-this-theatrical-naples-apartment){: target="_blank"} through the entrance, the villa's interior concept reveals itself: this is a home where clean architectural precision meets organic fluidity, where each curve and contour has been meticulously considered.

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/67ee32f9931c635e2e7652f2]

[#image: /photos/67ee32ff931c635e2e7652f4]

+++

[Set within Dubai's prestigious Jumeirah Golf Estates](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/the-most-beautiful-homes-in-dubai){: target="_blank"}, the villa’s angular structural elements are softened by flowing lines, pristine white volumes warmed by textural finishes, and dramatic statement features balanced with intimate, cocooning spaces. “The vision was to create a seamless blend of modern minimalism and luxury,” explain the designers.

The property was redeveloped for sale, but the design was carefully tailored to meet the lifestyle of its future residents. “We envisioned discerning buyers who value both style and functionality – individuals who prioritize family connections and social gatherings while enjoying a serene living environment,” they explain. Spacious hosting areas were designed to encourage connection and flow, with open-plan living spaces [flooded with natural light](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/manhattan-apartment-exposed-brick-ceiling-transformation-cosy-cocoon-power-of-lighting){: target="_blank"} that create smooth transitions between indoor and outdoor environments. The result is a home that feels elevated yet welcoming – a space where residents can truly feel connected to both their surroundings and the people they hold dear.



[#externallink: /externallinks/6503041abb917946d989f4c1]



[#image: /photos/67ee34db22de03245a8912f3]

Completed in late 2024, it spans 10,200 square meters, including a grand entrance to announce the property’s grandeur. A custom-crafted pivot door leads into a dramatic [double-height vestibule where the staircase reveals itself](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/warm-minimalism-design-ideas-greg-natale){: target="_blank"}. Handmade in Finland by Secto Design, a cascade of pendant lighting fixtures hangs at varying heights above the staircase, accentuating the vertical volume while filling the space with warm illumination.



[#image: /photos/67ee350322de03245a8912f5]

“One of the biggest challenges in executing the curvilinear elements was integrating them with the angularity of the villa's structural shell,” shares Singh. “We collaborated closely with contractors and craftsmen to develop custom templates, allowing us to achieve smooth, [uninterrupted curves](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/this-new-york-penthouse-fuses-craft-with-urban-style-for-a-cosy-chic-sanctuary){: target="_blank"} that felt intentional rather than out of place.”



[#image: /photos/67ee3526931c635e2e7652fc]



[#image: /photos/67ee356b22de03245a8912f7]

The villa achieves biophilic harmony without resorting to the expected tropes of living walls or overt greenery. Instead, it's the architectural language itself – the organic curves, the natural flow of spaces – that creates a profound connection to natural principles. At the heart is [a Zen-inspired](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/zen-interior-design-joanna-parol){: target="_blank"} courtyard – a grounding visual axis visible from key vantage points throughout. It serves as a conceptual anchor that connects disparate areas of the home while reinforcing the property's connection to nature.



[#image: /photos/67ee35bc22de03245a8912f9]

“We saw the courtyard as an opportunity to [soften the villa's architectural framework](https://www.admiddleeast.com/story/a-modern-farmhouse-look-home-in-dubai-jumeirah-golf-estates-contemporary-meets-rustic-interior){: target="_blank"},” explains Sahney. “The planting scheme was deliberately restrained, focusing on texture, height, and movement. The layering of plants creates a quiet rhythm, while subtle lighting highlights their form, adding depth throughout the day.” The result is a courtyard that feels integrated yet impactful – a serene focal point from which the villa’s architectural components “grow”.



[#image: /photos/67ee35e522de03245a8912fb]

Every aspect of the interior was meticulously curated to create a symbiotic relationship between light and space. Natural light floods the interior through expansive glazing, strategically positioned to maximize views of the surrounding landscape. The carefully considered placement of windows allows light to reflect off textured surfaces, creating a dynamic play of shadow and illumination that transforms spaces throughout the day. Outside, the organic, freeform pool echoes the villa's curvaceous architecture, creating a cohesive visual identity that extends beyond the built environment.



[#image: /photos/67ee361e931c635e2e7652fe]



[#image: /photos/67ee34a7931c635e2e7652fa]

When asked about their favorite design element, both designers highlight the master bathroom. “Positioned at the back of the property, it could have easily been overlooked, but we intentionally designed it as a hidden sanctuary,” they explain. “Upon entering, you're immediately enveloped in a serene, cave-like atmosphere that evokes the calm of a private spa.” The material palette is soft yet rich in texture, with natural stone surfaces that feel grounding and elegant. Floor-to-ceiling glazing enhances the sense of calm while maintaining privacy. “The interplay of light and shadow throughout the day creates a dynamic yet tranquil energy – a space that feels restorative and indulgent without being ostentatious.”



[#image: /photos/67ee367f22de03245a8912ff]



[#image: /photos/67ee366422de03245a8912fd]

The selection of materials speaks to a refined, tactile luxury. Interior flooring is clad in wide-plank Khars timber, lending warmth and elegance to the spaces. In contrast, textured microcement walls in the bathrooms and hallways create a monolithic feel. Porcelain tiles from Taf House introduce a play of texture in high-traffic areas, chosen for both durability and aesthetic appeal. Meanwhile, the villa's external façade is treated with a mix of contemporary cladding materials and soft stucco finishes, providing a subtly textured appearance that blends with the surrounding landscape.



[#image: /photos/67ee36a422de03245a891301]

The interior furnishings were curated with meticulous attention to both aesthetics and practicality. Standout items include bespoke seating from Tacchini and Ferm Living, which echo the villa's curvilinear architecture. The dining area is anchored by a sculptural, handcrafted table, while luxury accessories from brands like Louis Vuitton and 101 Copenhagen add refined accents throughout the space.



[#image: /photos/67ee36e622de03245a891305]

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/67ee36b2931c635e2e765300]

[#image: /photos/67ee36d122de03245a891303]

+++

“Each piece was selected or custom-made to fit seamlessly into the villa's design scheme,” notes Singh. “We aimed for an atmosphere of calm elegance, where every corner whispers luxury and refinement.” This philosophy of restrained sophistication extends beyond furnishings to every aspect of the home. Even the technology throughout this modern Dubai villa residence adheres to this principle of elegant discretion. State-of-the-art home automation systems remain virtually invisible, with sophisticated smart controls for lighting, climate, and entertainment integrated so seamlessly that they enhance the living experience without disrupting the visual tranquility.



[#image: /photos/67ee370722de03245a891307]

What stands out is the designers’ ability to balance contradictory elements: structural precision with organic fluidity, minimalist restraint with subtle lavishness, technological sophistication with a feel of complete serenity. “The architectural brief demanded maximizing both vertical and horizontal circulation while maintaining spatial fluidity,” explains Sahney. “Careful detailing ensured that structural elements became part of the aesthetic narrative rather than hindrances to it.”

title: Scarlett Johansson achète un appartement à 13 millions de dollars
content:
Scarlett Johansson et Colin Jost, mariés depuis 2020, ont un nouvel endroit charmant où poser leurs valises dans la [Grosse Pomme](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/charlotte-gainsbourg-appartement-new-york-v2). Selon le *Wall Street Journal*, l’actrice nommée aux Oscars et l’humoriste du *Saturday Night Live* ont déboursé 13 millions de dollars (11,85 millions d’euros) pour un penthouse d’avant-guerre dans le quartier de Carnegie Hill à Manhattan. D’une superficie de 557 mètres carrés, ce bien en copropriété dispose de six chambres et sept salles de bains. Les anciens propriétaires l’ont mis en vente pour la première fois en 2018 avec un prix demandé de 27,5 millions de dollars (25 millions de dollars) – le moins que l’on puisse dire, c’est que Scarlett Johansson et Colin Jost ont fait une bonne affaire. La dernière vente de ce logement de 14 pièces remonte à 2006, également pour 13 millions de dollars.

Réparti sur trois étages, l’appartement dispose d’un nombre impressionnant d’espaces extérieurs, avec cinq terrasses et huit [balcons](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/idees-balcons-havre-paix). Six cheminées à bois ornent l’unité, qui repose sur une structure des années 1930 conçue par Rosario Candela.

Un ascenseur privé s’ouvre sur une galerie centrale baignée de lumière naturelle, grâce à des portes-fenêtres allant du sol au plafond qui s’ouvrent sur un balcon Juliette. Le vaste salon adjacent présente des sols en parquet [Versailles](https://www.admagazine.fr/article/tout-savoir-chateau-versailles), des murs enduits de plâtre vénitien, une cheminée en marbre blanc et six autres balcons Juliette. La salle à manger formelle est dotée de sols à chevrons et de murs recouverts de peau de chèvre, selon l’annonce.

De l’autre côté de la galerie se trouve une grande bibliothèque en bois de chêne, avec une alcôve confortable bordée d’étagères, un accès à la terrasse et une vue sur Central Park. L’étage inférieur est complété par une salle d’eau et une cuisine bien équipée qui semble s’étendre sur des kilomètres et qui se termine par une salle de [petit-déjeuner](https://www.admagazine.fr/lifestyle/le-carnet-adresses-de/diaporama/cyril-lignac-devoile-sa-recette-de-petit-dejeuner-healthy/60567) à travers une série de portes de poche en bronze et en verre. Les deux niveaux supérieurs sont consacrés aux chambres et aux salles de bains ; la suite principale s’étend sur tout le dernier étage et comprend une vaste terrasse.

Article initialement publié dans [*AD* US](https://www.architecturaldigest.com/story/scarlett-johansson-and-colin-jost-buy-manhattan-penthouse){: target="_blank"}.

title: Saint Laurent e Anthony Vaccarello per la Milano Design week celebrano il genio di Charlotte Perriand, riportando alla luce quattro pezzi inediti
content:
**Saint Laurent per la Milano Design Week celebra Charlotte Perriand.**

Certe creazioni che ha disegnato per [Saint Laurent](https://www.ad-italia.it/gallery/casa-yves-saint-laurent-tangeri-hotel-villa-mabrouka/), con quelle silhouette forti e strutturate, richiamano le [geometrie](https://www.ad-italia.it/article/casa-stile-eclettico-genova-pareti-colorate/) e il rigore delle costruzioni più moderne. D’altronde Anthony Vaccarello, dal 2016 direttore creativo di una delle maison più celebri al mondo, non ha mai fatto segreto del suo amore per l’architettura, che si riflette nel suo lavoro, anzi.



[#image: /photos/67f3e88bd7b493cd8e2d8949]|||*Fauteuil Visiteur Indochine* (1943). Tutti i pezzi sono in mostra al Padiglione Visconti di Milano fino al 13 aprile.|||

## Una visione che unisce minimalismo e ricerca della bellezza
La sua è un’estetica chiara, determinata, ancorata al know-how, proiettata nel futuro. Facile stabilire delle connessioni tra lo stilista belga di origini italiane e la grande progettista francese che ha segnato la storia del design del XX secolo, Charlotte Perriand. La visione che unisce minimalismo e ricerca della bellezza nella semplicità è la stessa. Se Perriand ha rivoluzionato la progettazione degli interni e dei mobili, creando pezzi innovativi utilizzando materiali industriali come acciaio e alluminio, Vaccarello applica una filosofia molto simile nelle sue collezioni, sperimentando con volumi, tagli e tessuti. Un processo che si traduce nell’eleganza delle forme essenziali e nella funzionalità integrata al design, sia che si tratti di un arredo o di un abito.



[#image: /photos/67f3ea75447ec3f1201ddb53]|||La libreria *Bibliothèque Rio de Janeiro* (1962), uno dei quattro pezzi protagonisti alla Milano Design Week. sotto Anthony Vaccarello, direttore creativo di Saint Laurent e curatore della mostra.|||

## La mostra *Saint Laurent - Charlotte Perriand* al Padiglione Visconti 
Questa connessione tra moda e design trova un’espressione straordinaria nella mostra *Saint Laurent - Charlotte Perriand*, di scena alla [Milano Design Week](https://www.ad-italia.it/salone-del-mobile/) al Padiglione Visconti e curata dallo stesso Vaccarello. L’esposizione celebra la prima collaborazione tra Saint Laurent e l’heritage della leggendaria designer, presentando quattro inediti capolavori di Perriand (realizzati tra il 1943 e il 1967), rieditati in edizione limitata e disponibili solo su ordinazione. Questi pezzi – libreria, divano, sedute e tavolino –, fino a ora esistiti solo come bozzetti dimenticati negli archivi, o in edizioni singole di proprietà private, vengono finalmente riportati alla luce grazie a un accurato processo di sviluppo. Una partnership che celebra così, simbolicamente, l’incontro tra due mondi creativi. Esteticamente bellissimi.



[#image: /photos/67f3ea9314b2f5b78ed4022d]|||*Canapé de la Résidence de l’Ambassadeur du Japon* di Charlotte Perriand, Parigi, 1967.|||



[#image: /photos/67f3eab0d7b493cd8e2d894b]|||*Table Mille-Feuilles* (1963).|||



[#image: /photos/67f3eaaa5126f7eebe3390a2]|||Ritratto di Anthony Vaccarello.|||

title: 7 sustainable architecture insights for India from BIG's iconic projects
content:
“In *Formgivi*ng. *An Architectural Future History*, Bjarke Ingels writes that as architects, we must give something back to society,” begins Kai-Uwe Bergmann, partner at the globally renowned Bjarke Ingels Group (BIG). The ethos of sustainable architecture (an overused and often abused encapsulation of a branch of architecture that leaves much to interpretation) is being delightfully reshaped by BIG—moving away from a paradigm of limitation to one of opportunity. From hybrid power plants to community-driven [public parks](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/ahmedabads-parimal-garden-offers-a-poignant-reflection-of-the-city-since-the-1950s-prabhakar-bhagwat-landscape-architecture/){: target="_blank"}, the design studio has long championed the idea that sustainability should not only be functional—but also joyful, social, and deeply contextual.



[#image: /photos/67f4d7af2f0b5ef9e021a55e]|||*A visual render of Telosa.*|||

In conversation with *AD* India, Bergmann elaborates on this understanding of the social and sustainable architecture that drives BIG’s iconic projects, each of them offering not only a design strategy but a philosophical framework that can be meaningfully applied across the globe, and particularly to India's diverse, rapidly urbanising context.

## Make Sustainability Stick
Before studying their projects, it is important to note how BIG’s understanding of social sustainability guides them. As Bergmann points out, the classical understanding of the term innately involves some sense of limiting oneself, or spelling out a reduction in the quality of life for the greater good. BIG’s approach of enhancing through sustainability is different, one they have aptly termed ‘Hedonistic Sustainability’, and it begins with a simple question: *In what world do we actually want to live?*



[#image: /photos/67f4d81462d1bebe58149915]|||*Superkilen, an urban landscape park in Denmark.*|||

“There is a greater chance that people will change their habits if sustainability contributes to improving everyone’s lives. I believe this is also true in India, where the greater populace would rather move towards something that increases the quality of their daily life than limiting their choices,” Bergmann rightly points out. It is this sense of collective, communal joy that BIG seeks to channel through the power of design.

## CopenHill, Copenhagen: Infrastructure as an Educational Experience


[#image: /photos/67f4d64562d1bebe5814990f]|||*Copenhill in Copenhagen presents an industrial plant reimagined as a leisure hub.*|||

In the outskirts of Copenhagen, CopenHill is a power plant designed to incinerate waste and convert it into clean energy for 150,000 homes, while simultaneously offering a [lush green rooftop](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/a-vastu-compliant-garden-home-in-mumbai-with-a-2200-square-foot-terrace/){: target="_blank"} replete with community functions—presenting a hybrid industrial plant and leisure park. “The concept includes a didactic strand: once onsite, visitors find out how waste incinerator plants function, an important part of a functioning society,” Bergmann elaborates.



[#image: /photos/67f4d63e62d1bebe5814990d]|||*Copenhill, Copenhagen*|||

With India’s pace of growth, there exists an enormous need for future energy sources. While these should primarily come from renewables, Bergmann speaks about the possibility of using recycling, upcycling and new technologies to create additional energy loops. Urban India urgently needs to shift public perception around waste and energy and projects like CopenHill show that essential infrastructure doesn’t need to be hidden—it can be celebrated, raising environmental literacy through integrating learning and leisure opportunities.

## Google Bay View Campus: Harnessing Solar Power


[#image: /photos/67f4d69e0dddd9765880c120]|||*The Google Bay View campus in California.*|||

The shimmering, dragon-scale roof of the sprawling Google Bay View Campus in Silicon Valley, California (designed by BIG—Bjarke Ingels Group and [Heatherwick Studios](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/you-wont-believe-work-gets-done-at-these-three-google-offices/){: target="_blank"} in close collaboration with Google) might resemble a sprawling piece of futuristic origami, but actually boasts a photovoltaic roof made of 50,000 solar modules that ripple across the surface, expected to produce 90% of the campus’ energy requirements. “When it comes to choosing a project’s structural material, we carefully examine in advance what makes the most structural sense onsite, precisely with regard to the energy concept or the building’s life cycle,” Bergmann explains.



[#image: /photos/67f4d69c0dddd9765880c11f]|||*The Google Bay View campus in California.*|||

In India, the abundance of sunlight during the summer months or rainwater during the monsoon season opens doors to immense possibilities. With the still-scarce adoption of techniques that harness these elemental powers, projects like the [Google Campus](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/content/googles-new-1-billion-london-hq-massive-dubbed-landscraper/){: target="_blank"} serve as a prescient reminder that technology can be reframed not as a clunky afterthought, but as an aesthetic gesture.

## Superkilen: Creation through Public Participation


[#image: /photos/67f4d6f262d1bebe58149911]|||*Superkilen, an urban landscape park in Denmark brought to life through public participation.*|||

“Architecture must not be despotic, but represent a dialogue with context, history, and tradition. It should be an osmotic process of exchange and reciprocal enrichment,” says Bergmann. A large proportion of BIG’s socially sustainable projects employ public participation as a driving force for change. In Superkilen, an urban landscape park in an ethnically diverse neighbourhoods in Denmark with 60 different nationalities, BIG invited citizens to nominate objects from their home countries—Moroccan fountains, Russian neon signs, Indian benches, etc—to incorporate into the park’s identity, lending a sense of ownership to this [communal space](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/5-indian-courtyards-that-truly-exemplify-indoor-outdoor-living/){: target="_blank"}.



[#image: /photos/67f4d6e12f0b5ef9e021a55c]|||*Superkilen, an urban landscape park in Denmark*|||

“By transforming public procedure into proactive proposition, we curated a park for the people, by the people—peer-to-peer design,” he says. In Indian cities teeming with plural, informal voices left out of formal planning, this serves as a stark reminder that participatory design can reclaim ideas of true democratic urbanism.

## BIG U: Climactic Resilience to Cultural Richness
In response to the devastation of Hurricane Sandy, BIG proposed the BIG U: a 16-kilometre-long flood protection system wrapping around Lower Manhattan. But it doesn’t just block water; it creates greenways, markets, [amphitheatres](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/this-pune-design-studio-marries-art-food-music-and-mindful-living-experiences/){: target="_blank"}, and open spaces for residents to use year-round. “Every place has a soul, a genius loci that must be listened to, understood, and interpreted,” emphasises Bergmann, that goes beyond function in architecture to the culture of the city.

With coastal cities in India facing increased climatic threats as development continues, planning goals need to go beyond utilitarian protection to adaptable, human-centric urban systems. The BIG U model offers a compelling reframing: climate responses as an opportunity for cultural activation.

## Telosa: Building Future Cities Consciously


[#image: /photos/67f4d7af62d1bebe58149913]|||*A visual render of Telosa.*|||

How does one go about building a city from scratch? [Telosa](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/step-into-a-dollar500-billion-city-of-the-future-dreamed-up-by-an-american-billionaire/){: target="_blank"}, a proposed city for five million residents in the American desert, designed with walkability, renewable energy, clean air, and shared equity as its founding principles serves as an example where even the economic model upon which the city is founded was rethought. The founder Marc Lore is laying the groundwork for an equitable urban environment, bolstered by BIG’s vision for accessible and healthy urban spaces.



[#image: /photos/67f4d8650dddd9765880c122]|||*A visual render of Telosa.*|||

“In India, the sheer magnitude of city-making that is necessary to keep up with its population growth means that entire neighbourhoods and cities are being planned informally, and don’t always take into consideration the many systems needed to sustain them. This means that they grow at such a speed that mobility systems can break down,” Bergmann cautions. Clean water resources, public transportation systems, and [renewable energy](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/sponsored/story/discover-how-this-brands-advanced-technology-is-shaping-contemporary-spaces/){: target="_blank"} sources are at the root of Telosa’s thinking and should also be considered India - as any project, no matter what its size, will tax the country’s already exhausted systems.

## Drawing Lessons from the Past
Bergmann reminds us that sustainability was inherent to Indian architecture (shaded courtyards, generously laid-out [stepwells](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/a-stepwell-in-sudi-will-be-resorted-to-its-former-glory-by-the-deccan-heritage-foundation/){: target="_blank"}, evaporative cooling through public ponds of water benefiting the society, not just the patron who built them, and more). He also references dharamshalas in India. “I was always taken by these structures, which are simple, pragmatic, and well placed in the centres of towns to offer any passing traveler a place to rest their feet and meet local inhabitants,” he reveals. For India, where social sustainability was once such an intrinsic part of local architecture, it might just be a better matter of getting back to the roots.



[#image: /photos/67f4da230dddd9765880c124]|||*Bjarke Ingels Group’s monograph, Formgiving. An Architectural Future History.*|||

Bergmann’s observations and subsequent insights, from his research and time in India as a collaborator at [Conscious Collective](https://www.architecturaldigest.in/story/conscious-collective-by-godrej-and-boyce-heralds-innovations-in-sustainable-design/){: target="_blank"} by Godrej & Boyce, reveal that in the race to build bigger and better, there exists a prescient reminder for India that sustainable architecture isn’t limited to certifications and boxes to check—it needs to be a mindset that intersects imagination, empathy and equity.



[#cneembed: script/video/67dbd81942662d4a29f09882.js?aspectRatio=16x9]

title: Una villa al mare a pochi passi dalla spiaggia, con un giardino segreto avvolto dagli inebrianti profumi del Mediterraneo
content:
**Una villa al mare a pochi passi dalla spiaggia con un intimo giardino segreto.**

Un piccolo promontorio sulla costa meridionale della [**Corsica**](https://www.ad-italia.it/article/casa-corsica-collina-feng-shui-the-thinking-traveller/), a poco più di 20 km a nord-ovest di **Bonifacio**, e una serie ininterrotta di insenature e baie, molte delle quali con deliziose [spiaggette](https://www.ad-italia.it/gallery/migliori-spiagge-europa-dove-andare-questa-estate-vacanze/) di sabbia. Vicine e lontane, svettano le cime imponenti del **Massiccio de Cagna**, circondate da un vero e proprio paradiso per gli amanti della natura. Immersa in un’oasi di rigogliosa di vegetazione, a pochi passi da una *plage* quasi deserta, una villa sul mare affascina con le sue geometrie, invitanti illusioni ottiche che dissolvono e attenuano i confini tra spazi interni ed esterni. Sintineddu, autentico landmark di **Pianottoli**, ha qualcosa di famigliare, di autentico. E basta uno sguardo, un’occhiata, per riconoscere nei suoi tratti ciò che ha ispirato chi l’ha costruita. Il proprietario, un parigino, non ha mai dimenticato l’estetica raffinata e contemporanea di uno dei capolavori della designer irlandese [**Eileen Gray**](https://www.ad-italia.it/article/eileen-gray-casa-sul-mare-architettura-moderna-roquebrune-cap-martin/).



[#image: /photos/67f77e0e9f5218720c1988e2]



[#image: /photos/67f77e7b7544c3a6d1770529]

## La mitica E-1027
Era il 1926 quando il critico d’arte **Jean Badovici** con la compagna Eileen si innamorarono di **Cap Martin**, in **Provenza**, e progettarono la loro casa per le vacanze, la mitica **E-1027**, seguendo un codice che combinava i loro rispettivi nomi. Il proprietario di [**Sintineddu**](https://www.thethinkingtraveller.com/france/corsica/villas/sintineddu){: target="_blank"}, elegante [villa al mare](https://www.ad-italia.it/article/tabacchificio-abbandonato-masseria-puglia-casa-vacanza/) proposta da [**The Thinking Traveller**](https://www.thethinkingtraveller.com/){: target="_blank"}, ha seguito idealmente le loro tracce, attratto dalle bellezze del [**Mediterraneo**](https://www.ad-italia.it/article/da-casa-fatiscente-a-raffinato-rifugio-in-stile-mediterraneo-ristrutturazione/) e conquistato dalla magia architettonica del rifugio sulla scogliera di Gray e Badovici.



[#image: /photos/67f77e977544c3a6d177052b]

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/67f77ede7d83c86446ec5877]

[#image: /photos/67f77edee5d0dbc8db3307fa]

+++

Sintineddu abbaglia il visitatore con il suo bianco candido e un gioco di prospettive che ha nel paesaggio il suo baricentro. Sul lato affacciato sul mare, le terrazze e i [giardini](https://www.ad-italia.it/article/progettare-un-giardino-6-regole-oro-per-uno-spazio-outdoor-da-sogno/) regalano panorami spettacolari, mentre sul lato opposto un’area ombreggiata e appartata invita a rilassarsi in totale tranquillità. All’esterno, le forme bianche della villa spiccano tra il verde rigoglioso della vegetazione, creando un’atmosfera intima e serena. Davanti all’ingresso, accessibile grazie alle [vetrate scorrevoli](https://www.ad-italia.it/gallery/case-di-vetro-in-tutto-il-mondo/) della zona giorno, si apre una terrazza con vista mare, arredata con un tavolo da pranzo e incorniciata da strutture bianche che estendono il corpo principale della casa. Un paio di gradini conducono a una seconda *terrace*, arredata con lettini e circondata da prati e piante, seguita da un breve sentiero che ci accompagna fino alla suggestiva [piscina a sfioro](https://www.ad-italia.it/article/la-piscina-a-sfioro-piu-spettacolare-si-trova-in-un-hotel-con-vista-sulle-dolomiti/).



[#image: /photos/67f77f487544c3a6d177052d]

+++group-2

[#image: /photos/67f77f9e7544c3a6d177052f]

[#image: /photos/67f77f829f5218720c1988e4]

+++

## La magia di un giardino segreto
Sintineddu, che può ospitare fino a 10 persone, si sviluppa su due piani, con tre camere da letto situate al piano terra e accessibili dalle terrazze. Al centro del *rez de chaussée* si trova un luminoso living con due pareti di vetro scorrevoli, che si aprono da un lato sulle [terrazze vista mare](https://www.ad-italia.it/article/case-con-terrazzo-vista-mare-piu-belle-ad-italia/), sui giardini e la piscina, dall’altro sull’area esterna. I pavimenti, che si estendono anche sulle terrazze, le pareti bianche e le sedute di design regalano un’atmosfera sofisticata.



[#image: /photos/67f77fe67544c3a6d1770531]

La cucina, dall’elegante design e con una splendida vista panoramica, ha accesso diretto a entrambe le terrazze. Le armadiature a tutta altezza sono in [tonalità crema](https://www.ad-italia.it/article/pied-a-terre-new-york-city-colore-burro-tendenza-arredamento/), e avvolgono in un abbraccio il grande tavolo da pranzo centrale. Sullo stesso piano troviamo tre camere da letto, tutte indipendenti, con vista mozzafiato sul mare e sul giardino. La *master room*, che offre una vista spettacolare sul [Mediterraneo](https://www.ad-italia.it/article/le-straordinarie-case-del-mediterraneo-in-un-libro-gestalten-foto/) grazie a una parete interamente finestrata, si trova al primo piano ed è accessibile da una scala posizionata nel living. Il bagno *en-suite* include una [doccia](https://www.ad-italia.it/article/docce-piccole-consigli-arredamento/) walk-in e una vasca rialzata con vista mare.



[#image: /photos/67f780057544c3a6d1770533]

Una seconda vetrata scorrevole della zona giorno si apre su una seconda terrazza in parte murata, che costituisce il cuore nascosto della villa. Questo giardino segreto, che circonda un prato immacolato ed è ombreggiato dal tetto della casa e dai pini marittimi, ospita una zona soggiorno con vista mare elegantemente arredata e un grande [tavolo da pranzo](https://www.ad-italia.it/article/come-arredare-la-sala-da-pranzo-tendenze-2025/), servito direttamente dalla cucina. La terza meraviglia outdoor di Sintineddu è lo spazio panoramico al primo piano, accessibile direttamente dalla master bedroom o salendo l’elegante scalinata esterna. Da qui, la vista spazia dal mare alla costa ricoperta dalla macchia mediterranea, fino all’entroterra montagnoso dell’isola. È un luogo incantevole nel tardo pomeriggio, quando il sole inizia a tramontare nelle acque del [**Tirreno**](https://www.ad-italia.it/luoghi/itinerari/2020/08/07/calabria-da-visitare/).



[#image: /photos/67f7801de5d0dbc8db3307fc]



[#image: /photos/67f7805ae5d0dbc8db3307fe]

L’eleganza contemporanea e le accattivanti geometrie di Sintineddu, dove le pareti di vetro retrattili raccontano di un perfetto equilibrio fra architettura e bellezze naturali, sono un perfetto antidoto alla vita stressante di tutti i giorni. Niente di meglio di una tranquilla nuotata nella piscina o di una rigenerante passeggiata fino all’ospitale spiaggia di [sabbia](https://www.ad-italia.it/gallery/villa-arredamento-color-sabbia-robert-diaz-santa-monica-california-foto/). Prima di rilassarsi fra le bellezze della villa, circondati dalle opere di artisti contemporanei come **Hans Hartung** e **Alfred Hofkunst.**



[#image: /photos/67f780a07d83c86446ec5879]

### Leggi anche:
- [Questo appartamento ispirato allo stile scandinavo gioca con i volumi e i colori (chiari, naturalmente)](https://www.ad-italia.it/gallery/stile-scandinavo-appartamento-colori-chiari-tendenze/){: target="_blank"}
- [Una casa di famiglia che emoziona, un’isola del tesoro che rinasce nel verde della città](https://www.ad-italia.it/article/casa-famiglia-rinasce-verde-citta/){: target="_blank"}
